question: What is grace?
short_answer: Grace is God's favor shown to people who do not deserve it. It’s the means by which salvation is freely given, not earned through merit or obedience to the law.
expanded:
The concept of grace is central to understanding salvation in Christianity. According to Paul, as highlighted in Ephesians 2:8, we are saved “by grace,” meaning solely by God's favor and not by our own efforts. This emphasizes that salvation isn’t something we achieve but a gift freely bestowed upon us through faith.  Barnes notes this is a 'favorite doctrine with all who love the Lord Jesus in sincerity,' underscoring its importance.

Romans 3:24 further clarifies grace as justification “freely by his grace.” This means being declared righteous, or treated as if we had perfectly kept God’s law, despite our failings. JFB explains that this isn't a mere decree but is rooted in the 'redemption that is in Christ Jesus,' signifying the payment of a ransom for our sins.

The Old Testament Law, while important, served to reveal humanity's need for grace (John 1:17). It highlighted sin and inability to meet God’s standards. The Gospel, however, brings forth “the grace of God” (Titus 2:11), which has been manifested through Jesus Christ, offering a path to salvation that transcends the limitations of the Law.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: By mere favor. It is not by your Own merit; it is not because you have any claim. This is a favorite doctrine with Paul, as it is with all who love the Lord Jesus in sincerity.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: through faith—the effect of the power of Christ's resurrection…Christ alone is the meritorious agent.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Being justified - Being treated as if righteous; that is, being regarded and treated as if they had kept the Law.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: justified freely—without anything done on our part to deserve. by his grace—His free love. through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus—a most important clause; teaching us that though justification is quite gratuitous, it is not a mere fiat of the divine will, but based on a 'Redemption,' that is, 'the payment of a Ransom.'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The grace of God - The favor of God, shown to the undeserving.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: hath appeared—Greek, 'hath been made to appear,' or 'shine forth' (Isa 9:2; Lu 1:79).

question: What is salvation?
short_answer: Salvation is deliverance from harm, both physical and spiritual, ultimately referring to a rescue from sin. It's not earned through good works but received as a gift by grace through faith in Jesus Christ.
expanded:
The concept of salvation encompasses more than just physical preservation; it signifies deliverance from any form of evil, whether bodily or mental distress. However, within the New Testament, 'salvation' primarily refers to being rescued from sin – a work accomplished by the Messiah as stated in Luke 19:10 where He came “to seek and to save that which was lost.” This rescue involves deliverance from the power of sin and ultimately leads to eternal life.

The commentators emphasize that salvation is not achieved through human effort or merit. As Romans 3:24 notes, it's a gift received by grace, freely given, and accessed through faith. Titus 3:5 clarifies that our good works play no role in earning this salvation; rather, it’s an act of God’s mercy.

Furthermore, the commentary highlights that there is only one way to receive this salvation – through acknowledging Jesus Christ as Lord. Acts 4:12 states plainly, “Neither is there salvation in any other,” and Romans 10:9 explains that confession with your mouth, professing Jesus as Lord, is a vital component of receiving it.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The word 'salvation' properly denotes any 'preservation,' or keeping anything in a 'safe' state; a preserving from harm... This work refers primarily to a deliverance of the soul from sin.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: How sublimely does the apostle, in these closing words, shut up these rulers of Israel to Jesus for salvation, and in what universal and emphatic terms does he hold up his Lord as the one Hope of men!

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: It is a great and fundamental principle of the gospel that the good works of men come in for no share in the justification of the soul.

question: What is the gospel?
short_answer: The gospel, as Paul received and delivered it, is the message concerning Jesus Christ's death for our sins and resurrection, which he proclaims without shame because it is the power of God for salvation.
expanded:
According to Paul, the gospel is a foundational set of doctrines that he received directly from the Lord Jesus by inspiration. It represents a core belief system, likely even existing as a short creed among early Christians (JFB). A key element of this message is Christ's death “for our sins,” an act intended to atone for those sins (JFB), and his subsequent resurrection.

Paul emphasizes that proclaiming the gospel requires courage, as it was often met with opposition and rejection by both Jews and Gentiles. Despite facing persecution and being regarded as despised, Paul declares he is not ashamed of the gospel, glorifying it as “the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth” (JFB). He asserts that any message contradicting this established revelation from God would be false, even if seemingly delivered by an angel (JFB).

The apostle’s insistence on the gospel's importance underscores its central role in Christian faith and practice. It is not a doctrine of human origin but a divinely revealed truth, essential for salvation.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: For I delivered unto you - See the note at 1 Corinthians 11:23 . "First of all." Among the first doctrines which I preached. As the leading and primary doctrines of Christianity.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 3. I delivered unto you—A short creed, or summary of articles of faith, was probably even then existing; and a profession in accordance with it was required of candidates for baptism (Ac 8:37).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: He had so firm a conviction of its value and its truth; he had experienced so much of its consolations; and had seen so much of its efficacy; that he was so far from being ashamed of it that he gloried in it as the power of God unto salvation

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth—Here and in Ro 1:17 the apostle announces the great theme of his ensuing a

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But though we - That is, we the apostles. Probably, he refers particularly to himself, as the plural is often used by Paul when speaking of himself.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "Even though we," namely, I and the brethren with me, weighty and many as we are (Ga 1:1, 2).

question: What is repentance?
short_answer: Repentance involves sorrow for sins committed against God, accompanied by a firm purpose to forsake them and trust in the gospel.
expanded:
The concept of repentance is central to salvation and Christian living. According to Peter, it means “change of mind” and includes accepting the Gospel as its natural outcome (Acts 2:38). It's not merely fearing punishment but rather recognizing sin as inherently evil, leading to a hatred of it and a desire to turn away from it (Barnes on Acts 2:38).

True repentance is described as “godly sorrow,” which aligns with God’s will and draws the soul closer to Him (Barnes on 2 Corinthians 7:10). This sorrow 'worketh out' repentance, contrasting with worldly sorrow that ultimately leads to death (JFB on 2 Corinthians 7:10).

The parable of the prodigal son illustrates a key aspect of repentance: returning to God despite past offenses and seeking reconciliation (Luke 15:18). Even after treating his father unkindly, the son felt he could still trust in his kindness and seek forgiveness. This highlights the availability of grace and restoration for those who repent.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Repentance implies sorrow for sin as committed against God, along with a purpose to forsake it. It is not merely a fear of the consequences of sin or of the wrath of God in hell.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: The word [repent] denotes change of mind, and here includes the reception of the Gospel as the proper issue of that revolution of mind which they were then undergoing.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This is a very important expression in regard to true repentance, and shows the exact nature of that sorrow which is connected with a return to God.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Sorrow' is not repentance, but, where it is 'godly,' 'worketh' it; that is, contributes or tends to it.

question: What is faith?
short_answer: Faith is described as "the substance of things hoped for," meaning it provides confidence and assurance regarding promises yet to be fulfilled. It’s also presented as the instrument through which God's grace, freely given, leads to salvation.
expanded:
Hebrews 11:1 defines faith as “the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen.” Barnes explains this means it is a 'ground or confidence,' providing assurance about future realities. JFB elaborates that it substantiates God’s promises, making them present realities to believers. This isn't merely intellectual assent but an active trust.

The passage in Ephesians 2:8 clarifies that salvation comes through faith, which is a gift from God and not earned by human merit.  JFB notes that this faith acts as the instrument of salvation, facilitated by Christ’s power. It emphasizes that while faith is essential, it's not something we generate ourselves but rather received.

Romans 10:17 highlights how faith arises through hearing God's word. This underscores the importance of exposure to divine revelation for cultivating belief. James 2:19 cautions against a mere intellectual acknowledgment of God’s existence, pointing out that even demons possess such knowledge without possessing true faith – which requires action and demonstration.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Now faith is the substance of things hoped for - On the general nature of faith, see the notes on Mark 16:16 . The margin here is, "ground or confidence."

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: substance, &c.—It substantiates promises of God which we hope for, as future in fulfilment, making them present realities to us.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This is a favorite doctrine with Paul, as it is with all who love the Lord Jesus in sincerity; compare the notes at Romans 1:7 ; Romans 3:24 , note.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: through faith—the effect of the power of Christ's resurrection (Eph 1:19, 20; Php 3:10) whereby we are "raised together" with Him (Eph 2:6; Col 2:12).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Even by this passage, therefore, it seems that a message was necessary, that faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the divine message.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "This is another confirmation of the truth that faith supposes the hearing of the Word, and this a commission to preach it."

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The design of the apostle seems to have been to select one of the doctrines of religion, the belief of which would - if mere belief in any doctrine could - save the soul; and to show that even this might be held as an article of faith by those who could be supposed by no one to have any claim to the name of Christian.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "Thou self-deceiving claimant to faith without works. that there is one God—rather, 'that God is one': God's existence, however, is also asserted."

question: What is sin?
short_answer: Sin is defined as falling short of God's glory and failing to meet His approval. It involves transgression against God’s law and a departure from righteousness.
expanded:
The concept of sin permeates both Pauline and Johannine theology, fundamentally representing a deficiency in relation to God. Romans 3:23 states that “all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God,” meaning all individuals fail to attain divine approval through their own efforts. This failure is not merely about breaking rules but about missing the mark of righteousness and experiencing God's favor.

The apostle John further clarifies that sin is intrinsically linked to transgression against God’s law (1 John 3:4).  He emphasizes that those who commit sin are violating a divine standard, demonstrating a lack of connection with God. Conversely, true believers strive for holiness, reflecting the character of Christ and actively avoiding sinful actions.

The consequences of this initial state of sinfulness are profound, as illustrated in Romans 5:12.  This passage highlights how sin entered the world through Adam and has been passed down to all humanity, creating a pervasive condition of guilt and separation from God. However, it also introduces the hope of redemption through Christ, who bore the penalty for our sins.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This was the point which he had fully established in the chapters. Have come short - Greek, 'Are deficient in regard to;' are lacking, etc. Here it means, that they had failed to obtain, or were destitute of. The glory of God - The praise or approbation of God.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: for all have sinned—Though men differ greatly in the nature and extent of their sinfulness, there is absolutely no difference between the best and the worst of men, in the fact that 'all have sinned,' and so underlie the wrath of God.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The object of the apostle here is to excite them to holiness, and to deter them from committing sin, perhaps in view of the fact stated in 1 John 3:3 , that everyone who has the hope of heaven will aim to be holy like the Saviour.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Whosoever—Greek, 'Every one who.' committeth sin—in contrast to 1Jo 3:3, 'Every man that hath this hope in Him purifieth himself.'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: All we, like sheep, have gone astray - This is the penitent confession of those for whom he suffered. It is an acknowledgment that they were going astray from God.

question: What is atonement?
short_answer: Atonement refers to Christ's sacrifice which satisfies God’s justice and removes the barrier between humanity and God caused by sin. It is described as 'a propitiation for our sins,' meaning He became a substitute, bearing the penalty we deserved.
expanded:
The concept of atonement centers on the idea that humanity's sin creates an offense against God’s righteousness, requiring a sacrifice to restore relationship. Paul emphasizes this in 2 Corinthians 5:21, stating that Christ “hath made him to be sin for us,” embodying and concentrating all reasons for reconciliation with God. This isn't simply about offering a sacrifice; it involves Christ taking on the role of 'Sin-bearer,' vicariously absorbing the consequences of our actions.

The term 'propitiation,' as used in 1 John 2:2, signifies that Jesus is “the propitiation,” removing God’s righteous wrath against sinners. This appeasement isn't a change of heart on God’s part but rather the satisfying of justice through Christ’s sacrifice.

The prophet Isaiah foretold this suffering in Isaiah 53:5, describing how Christ would be “wounded for our transgressions” and “bruised for our iniquities.” This highlights the physical pain and suffering endured by Jesus as a direct consequence of bearing the sins of others. Romans 3:25 further clarifies that God 'set forth' Christ to be this propitiation, demonstrating His plan for reconciliation through faith in His blood.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The design of this very important verse is, to urge the strongest possible reason for being reconciled to God. This is implied in the word (γὰρ gar) 'for.' Paul might have urged other arguments... But he chooses to present this fact, that Christ has been made sin for us, as embodying and concentrating all.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: The grand reason why they should be reconciled to God, namely, the great atonement in Christ provided by God, is stated without the 'for' as being part of the message of reconciliation (2Co 5:19).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The word rendered 'propitiation' (ἱλασμός hilasmos) occurs nowhere else in the New Testament, except in 1 John 4:10 of this Epistle; though words of the same derivation, and having the same essential meaning, frequently occur.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "There is no incongruity that a father should be offended with that son whom he loveth, and at that time offended with him when he loveth him" [Bishop Pearson].

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The word properly means, 'to place in public view;' to exhibit in a conspicuous situation, as goods are exhibited or exposed for sale, or as premiums or rewards of victory were exhibited to public view in the games of the Greeks.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: to declare his righteousness for the remission—rather, 'pretermission' or 'passing by.' of sins—'the sins.'

question: Who is the Holy Spirit?
short_answer: The Holy Spirit is often referred to as another Comforter or Advocate, who intercedes on behalf of believers and dwells within them. He is the Spirit of God, producing virtues like love, joy, peace, gentleness, goodness, faith, meekness, and temperance.
expanded:
The commentary reveals that the Holy Spirit is a multifaceted figure crucial to Christian theology. John's Gospel describes Him as “another Comforter,” an advocate or helper who follows Christ (John 14:16). This 'Comforter' intercedes for believers before God, and this function is also described in Romans 8:34 and Hebrews 4:14-15.

The Apostle Paul emphasizes that the Holy Spirit dwells within Christians, distinguishing them from those living under the influence of sinful desires (Romans 8:9). He clarifies that being 'in the spirit' signifies being under the dominion of a renewed mind, evidenced by the indwelling of God’s Spirit. This presence is not merely an intellectual understanding but a transformative power.

The revelation of Ananias' deception in Acts 5:3 highlights the Holy Spirit's ability to reveal hidden truths and expose wickedness. Peter knew of Ananias’ deceit through direct revelation from God, demonstrating the Spirit’s role in divine guidance and judgment. Furthermore, Galatians 5:22 illustrates that the Holy Spirit produces a 'fruit' – a collection of virtues—that are not inherent within human nature but rather result from His influence.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This refers to his intercession after his death and ascension to heaven, for this prayer was to be connected with their keeping his commandments. It is as the result of his intercession in heaven that we obtain all our blessings, and it is through him that our prayers are to be presented and made efficacious before God.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Its proper sense is an 'advocate,' 'patron,' 'helper.' In this sense it is plainly meant of Christ (1Jo 2:1), and in like manner, here, of the Holy Spirit.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The Holy Spirit is often represented as dwelling in the hearts of Christians...and that they are under the direction and influence of the Holy Spirit.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 'if any man have not the Spirit of Christ—Again, this does not mean 'the disposition or mind of Christ,' but the Holy Ghost; here called 'the Spirit of Chris.'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The vices above enumerated are the proper 'works' or result of the operations of the human heart; the virtues which he enumerates are produced by a foreign influence - the agency of the Holy Spirit.

question: What is prayer?
short_answer: Prayer, according to these passages, is a manner of communicating with God, expressing our needs and concerns while trusting in His provision. It involves both specific requests and general thanksgiving, freeing the mind from anxiety and relying on divine assistance.
expanded:
The Lord's Prayer, as presented in Matthew 6:9, serves as a model for how we should approach God in prayer, demonstrating comprehensiveness and beauty. While not intended to be recited verbatim, it illustrates the substance of what genuine prayer entails – expressing needs and desires while acknowledging God’s sovereignty. JFB emphasizes that this prayer was given “as a form,” designed to show how much real prayer could be compressed into few words.

Philippians 4:6 highlights a crucial aspect of prayer: it is an alternative to anxiety. Rather than being burdened by worries, believers are encouraged to bring their concerns to God through prayer and thanksgiving. This act of entrusting our needs to Him releases us from the grip of worry and fosters a sense of calm dependence.

The Spirit's role in prayer, as described in Romans 8:26, further illuminates its nature. When we struggle to articulate our deepest desires or understand what is truly needed, the Holy Spirit intercedes on our behalf, assisting us with divine guidance. This underscores the idea that prayer isn’t solely about formulating perfect petitions but about opening ourselves to God's will and allowing Him to speak through us.

Finally, James 5:16 emphasizes the importance of confessing faults one to another as part of a life of prayer.  This act of humility and seeking counsel can contribute to healing and restoration, both spiritually and physically.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This passage contains the Lord's prayer, a composition unequalled for comprehensiveness and for beauty...It is designed to express the "manner" in which we are to pray, evidently not the precise words or petitions which we are to use.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Did it consist only of hints or directions for prayer, it could only be used as a directory; but seeing it is an actual prayer—designed, indeed, to show how much real prayer could be compressed into the fewest words, but still, as a prayer, only the more incomparable for that.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Be careful for nothing - That is, be not anxious or solicitous about the things of the present life...but that there is to be such confidence in God as to free the mind from anxiety, and such a sense of dependence on him as to keep it calm.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Care and prayer are as mutually opposed as fire and water [Bengel].

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Confess your faults one to another - This seems primarily to refer to those who were sick, since it is added, "that ye may be healed."

question: How should I pray?
short_answer: The Lord's Prayer serves as a model for how we should pray, emphasizing both substance and manner. We are to approach God with confidence, bringing our requests and anxieties to Him through prayer and thanksgiving.
expanded:
According to Matthew 6:9, the Lord’s Prayer is presented not as a rigid formula but as a “model” demonstrating the proper way to pray. While some petitions may have been drawn from Jewish traditions, their combination in this prayer creates something unique and beautiful. Luke 11:1 further clarifies that Jesus' disciples were struck by both the content and fervor of His prayers, prompting them to ask Him to teach them how to pray – highlighting the importance of observing a Godly example.

Philippians 4:6 instructs us not to be anxious about anything but instead to bring our requests to God through prayer and thanksgiving. This doesn't mean neglecting practical care for ourselves or our families, but rather trusting in God’s provision and maintaining a calm spirit. Luke 18:1 reinforces the need for perseverance in prayer, urging us not to grow weary even if answers seem delayed.

The parable of the importunate widow (Luke 18:1) illustrates that persistent and heartfelt prayer will ultimately be heard.  Barnes notes this parable is designed to teach that we must “always maintain a spirit of prayer.” Ultimately, approaching God with humility, gratitude, and unwavering faith is key.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This passage contains the Lord's prayer, a composition unequalled for comprehensiveness and for beauty...It is designed to express the “manner” in which we are to pray, evidently not the precise words or petitions which we are to use.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Did it consist only of hints or directions for prayer, it could only be used as a directory; but seeing it is an actual prayer—designed, indeed, to show how much real prayer could be compressed into the fewest words, but still, as a prayer, only the more incomparable for that—it is strange that there should be a doubt whether we ought to pray that very prayer.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: That is, be not anxious or solicitous about the things of the present life...but that there is to be such confidence in God as to free the mind from anxiety, and such a sense of dependence on him as to keep it calm.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Care and prayer are as mutually opposed as fire and water [Bengel].

question: What does it mean to abide in Christ?
short_answer: To abide in Christ means to remain united with Him through living faith, obedience to His teachings, and dependence on Him. In return, Christ promises to dwell within believers, guiding and comforting them.
expanded:
The concept of abiding in Christ is central to the Christian life, signifying a deep and continuous relationship with Jesus. According to Barnes, it involves “remaining united to me by a living faith,” meaning more than just intellectual assent; it's about actively obeying His doctrines and imitating His example.

The commentators emphasize that this mutual abiding – Christ in believers and believers in Christ – is essential for spiritual fruitfulness. JFB explains that it’s an “active interpenetration” where Christ’s words dwell within, shaping thoughts, affections, and actions. This allows believers to ask for things aligned with God's will.

Furthermore, abiding in Christ isn't merely about remembering His teachings but allowing them to regulate one's life. Barnes notes that it means “suffering [Christ’s] words to remain in you as a living principle.”  This also implies mirroring Christ’s actions and character; 1 John 2:6 states, "ought himself also so to walk, even as he walked." Ultimately, abiding is about continuing the initial reception of Christ – receiving Him as Lord and walking consistently with that faith, as Colossians 2:6 urges.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Abide in me - Remain united to me by a living faith. Live a life of dependence on me, and obey my doctrines, imitate my example, and constantly exercise faith in me.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: As all spiritual fruitfulness had been ascribed to the mutual inhabitation, and living, active interpenetration (so to speak) of Christ and His disciples...

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: My words - My doctrine; my commandments. Abide in you - Not only are remembered, but are suffered to remain in you as a living principle, to regulate your affections and life.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Mark the change from the inhabitation of Himself to that of His words, paving the way for the subsequent exhortations (Joh 15:9, 10).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: He that saith, he abideth in him - Greek, 'remains' in him; that is, abides or remains in the belief of his doctrines, and in the comfort and practice of religion.

question: What is justification?
short_answer: Justification is being treated as righteous in God's sight, not by personal merit or obedience to the law, but as a free gift through faith in Christ’s redemptive work.
expanded:
The concept of justification is central to understanding salvation. According to Paul, it involves being declared righteous before God, meaning we are regarded and treated as if we had perfectly kept His Law – despite our actual failings. This isn't earned through works or adherence to the law; rather, it’s a gift bestowed upon those who believe. As Barnes explains, “This is the essence of the gospel.”

The process of justification hinges on Christ’s sacrifice and redemption. JFB emphasizes that while gratuitous, this act is based on “a 'Redemption,' that is, 'the payment of a Ransom,' in Christ's death,” signifying God’s provision for our sin.

Romans 5:1 connects justification with peace with God, highlighting one of its blessed consequences.  Furthermore, 2 Corinthians 5:21 illustrates the profound nature of this act, stating that God “hath made him to be sin for us.” Ultimately, justification is extended even to those considered 'ungodly' (Romans 4:5), demonstrating God’s mercy and grace.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Being justified - Being treated as if righteous; that is, being regarded and treated as if they had kept the Law. The apostle has shown that they could not be so regarded and treated by any merit of their own, or by personal obedience to the Law. He now affirms that if they were so treated, it must be by mere favor, and as a matter not of right, but of gift.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: justified freely—without anything done on our part to deserve. by his grace—His free love. through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus—a most important clause; teaching us that though justification is quite gratuitous, it is not a mere fiat of the divine will, but based on a 'Redemption,' that is, 'the payment of a Ransom,' in Christ's death.

question: What is sanctification?
short_answer: Sanctification is God’s command for believers to become holy, progressing towards greater likeness to God and detachment from the world. It involves cleansing from sin and being set apart for a holy purpose.
expanded:
The concept of sanctification appears throughout scripture as an essential aspect of Christian life. Barnes explains that it's “the will or command of God” for believers, not simply His intention or decree. This isn’t about God making people holy against their will, but rather a call to obedience and a process where individuals strive towards holiness.

JFB elaborates on this progressive nature, describing sanctification as an advancement and completion of what has already begun in the believer's life. It involves being cleansed from sin and increasingly reflecting Christ’s glory, both spiritually now and with the promise of future bodily transformation. This process is facilitated “through [God's] truth,” specifically His revealed word.

Furthermore, sanctification isn’t solely an individual pursuit; it’s a transformative process that manifests outwardly. As described in 2 Corinthians 3:18, believers are changed into the same image as Christ, with unveiled faces reflecting God’s glory – a stark contrast to the veiled hearts of those who do not know Him.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This does not refer to the purpose or decree of God, and does not mean that he intended to make them holy - but it means that it was his command that they should be holy.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: It is positive, asking the advancement and completion of their begun sanctification.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Sanctification in the heart of a Christian is progressive. It consists in his becoming more like God and less attached to the world; in his getting the ascendency over evil thoughts, and passions, and impure desires; and in his becoming more and more weaned from earthly objects, and attached to those things which are unseen and eternal.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: This prayer, 'Sanctify them,' is positive, asking the advancement and completion of their begun sanctification.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: with unveiled face,” alluding to the fact 2 Corinthians 3:13 that the face of Moses was veiled, so that the children of Israel could not stedfastly look on it.

question: Why did Jesus die?
short_answer: Jesus died because he took upon himself the punishment for our sins, demonstrating God's love and fulfilling prophecies.
expanded:
The commentary highlights that Jesus’ death was a deliberate act of divine love. Romans 5:8 states that "God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us." This demonstrates God's willingness to sacrifice for humanity despite our sinful nature.

The suffering and death of Jesus are also connected to Old Testament prophecies, particularly Isaiah 53. Barnes notes that the passage speaks of someone “wounded” and “bruised,” which aligns with the physical pain endured by Christ on the cross. JFB elaborates that this wounding was "for… for—the cause for which He suffered not His own, but our sins."

Galatians 3:13 further clarifies that Jesus “hath redeemed us from its curse.” This redemption signifies liberation from the consequences of sin and death through his sacrifice. Finally, 1 Peter 3:18 emphasizes that Christ "also hath once suffered for sins, the Just for the unjust," underscoring his innocence and the voluntary nature of his suffering.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But he was wounded - Margin, 'Tormented.' Jerome and the Septuagint also render this, 'He was wounded.'

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: for … for—(Ro 4:25; 2Co 5:21; Heb 9:28; 1Pe 2:24; 3:18)—the cause for which He suffered not His own, but our sins.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Christ died for us - In our stead; to save us from death. He took our place; and by dying himself on the cross, saved us from dying eternally in hell.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "setteth off,” “displayeth”—in glorious contrast with all that men will do for each other. his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners—that is, in a state not of positive 'goodness,' nor even of negative 'righteousness.'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: For Christ also hath once suffered for sins - Compare the notes at 1 Peter 2:21 . The design of the apostle in the reference to the sufferings of Christ, is evidently to remind them that he suffered as an innocent being, and not for any wrong-doing.

question: What is the resurrection?
short_answer: The resurrection refers to Jesus Christ's rising from the dead, a pivotal event demonstrating his power over death and essential for believers’ salvation. It serves as a pledge that all who believe in him will also experience resurrection.
expanded:
The concept of the resurrection is central to Christian theology, particularly concerning the person and work of Jesus Christ. Paul emphasizes its importance, stating that if Christ was not raised, “your faith is vain” (1 Corinthians 15:17), implying a lack of pardon for sins and an invalidation of hope. The apostle proclaims, "But now is Christ risen from the dead" (1 Corinthians 15:20), expressing certainty based on personal experience and highlighting its significance as “the firstfruits” – a pledge that all believers will also be raised. 

The resurrection isn't merely a historical event; it’s intrinsically linked to baptism, which symbolizes being buried with Christ (Romans 6:4). This act signifies participation in his death and subsequent resurrection. Jesus himself declared, “I am the resurrection…and the life” (John 11:25), asserting his divine power over both death and eternal existence, promising that believers will ultimately overcome death through him.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Your faith is vain, - 1 Corinthians 15:14 . The meaning of this passage here is, that their faith was vain, 'because,' if Christ was not raised up, they were yet unpardoned sinners. The pardon of sin was connected with the belief of the resurrection of the Lord Jesus, and, if he was not raised, he was an impostor; and, of course, all your hopes of pardon by him, and through hi

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "The whole power to restore, impart, and maintain life, resides in Me." (See on [1832]Joh 1:4; [1833]Joh 5:21). What higher claim to supreme divinity than this grand saying can be conceived?

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: "But now is Christ risen ... - This language is the bursting forth of a full heart and of overpowering conviction. It would seem as if Paul were impatient of the slow process of argument; weary of meeting objections, and of stating the consequences of a denial of the doctrine; and longing to give utterance to 'what he knew,' that Christ was risen from the dead.'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Therefore we are buried ... - It is altogether probable that the apostle in this place had allusion to the custom of baptizing by immersion. This cannot, indeed, be proved, so as to be liable to no objection; but I presume that this is the idea which would strike the great mass of unprejudiced readers.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: I am the resurrection - I am the author or the cause of the resurrection. It so depends on my power and will, that it may be said that I am the resurrection itself.

question: What is the church?
short_answer: The church is a community of believers united by faith in Christ, characterized by steadfast adherence to His teachings and mutual dependence, much like the parts of a body.
expanded:
The concept of the church originates with Jesus's declaration to Peter, where He refers to him as “a rock” (Matthew 16:18). This play on words highlights that Peter, through his confession of faith, would be foundational. The Greek and Aramaic languages reveal a deeper understanding of this statement, emphasizing firmness, stability, and the significance of Christ’s identity.

The church is also described as being under the headship of Christ, who has dominion over all things for its benefit (Ephesians 1:22).  As JFB notes, “He is OVER all things; in contrast to the words, 'TO the Church,' namely, for her advantage.” This signifies that everything exists for the church’s welfare and is joined with Christ in His dominion.

Furthermore, Paul uses the analogy of a human body to illustrate the unity within the church (1 Corinthians 12:12).  Just as various parts of a body work together harmoniously, so too do believers with their diverse spiritual gifts. This emphasizes that while there is variety, there is also an organic wholeness and interdependence among all members.

The early church exemplified this unity through steadfast adherence to the apostles' teachings, fellowship, breaking of bread (likely communal meals), and prayer (Acts 2:42). Barnes observes that their perseverance despite facing persecution demonstrates a remarkable commitment.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: And they continued stedfastly - They persevered in, or they adhered to. This is the inspired record of the result. That any of these apostatized is nowhere recorded, and is not to be presumed.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "HIM He gave as Head over all things to the Church." Had it been anyone save Him, her Head, it would not have been the boon it is to the Church. But as He is Head over all things who is also her Head (and she the body), all things are hers.

question: What is baptism?
short_answer: Baptism signifies a renunciation of sin and a pledge to live for God, marking entry into Christ's death and symbolizing the putting on of Christ’s characteristics.
expanded:
The act of baptism is deeply significant in Christian faith. According to Romans 6:3, when individuals are baptized, they are symbolically united with Christ's death, signifying a renunciation of sin and a commitment to living for God. Barnes explains that this profession involves “all who were baptized; that is, all professed Christians,” demonstrating a pledge to live according to Christian principles.

Matthew 28:19 clarifies the practice, stating that baptism should be performed "into the name" of the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, emphasizing its role in making disciples. JFB notes that “teaching” is part of this process, coming after one is baptized.

Acts 2:38 connects baptism with repentance and remission of sins, signifying a change of mind and a visible seal of forgiveness through Jesus Christ. Galatians 3:27 further elaborates on the symbolism, stating that those who are baptized “have put on Christ,” adopting his traits and becoming sons by adoption, demonstrating that baptism is not merely an empty ritual but a transformative experience.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This is a further appeal to the Christian profession, and the principles involved in it, in answer to the objection. The simple argument in this verse and the two following is, that by our very profession made in baptism, we have renounced sin, and have pledged ourselves to live to God.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 3. Know ye not, that so many of us as were baptized into Jesus Christ…were baptized into his death?—sealed with the seal of heaven, and as it were formally entered and articled, to all the benefits and all the obligations of Christian discipleship in general, and of His death in particular.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Teach all nations - The word rendered 'teach,' here, is not the one that is usually so translated in the New Testament. This word properly means 'to disciple, or to make disciples of.'

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost—It should be, 'into the name'; as in 1Co 10:2, 'And were all baptized unto (or rather 'into') Moses'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: For as many of you - Whether by nature Jews or Gentiles. As have been baptized into Christ - Or 'unto' (εἰς eis) - the same preposition which in Galatians 3:24 is rendered unto) Christ.

question: What is the Lord's Supper?
short_answer: The Lord’s Supper is a sacred institution received directly from the Lord, designed as a commemoration of Christ’s death and a means by which believers dedicate themselves to His service.
expanded:
The Corinthians had misunderstood the nature of the Lord's Supper, turning it into a common festival marked by disorder. Paul sought to correct this by reminding them of its true purpose: to remember the Redeemer’s sacrifice. The apostle emphasized that he received the institution directly from the Lord through revelation, highlighting its solemnity and significance.

The act of partaking in the Lord's Supper is more than a simple meal; it's a corporate blessing where believers acknowledge Christ as their Lord and dedicate themselves to His service. This parallels the Jewish “cup of blessing” used during Passover, signifying a transition between old and new covenants.

Luke 22:19 describes Jesus taking the cup and declaring that it would be shared until the Kingdom of God arrives – a symbolic promise of future fellowship with Him in His Father’s kingdom. The Lord's Supper serves as a bridge connecting believers to Christ’s sacrifice and anticipating His return.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: In order most effectually to check the evils which existed, and to bring them to a proper mode of observing the Lord's Supper, the apostle proceeds to state distinctly and particularly its design. They had mistaken its nature. They supposed it might be a common festival. They had made it the occasion of great disorder.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: His object is to show the unworthiness of such conduct from the dignity of the holy supper... I—Emphatic in the Greek. It is not my own invention, but the Lord’s institution. received of the Lord—by immediate revelation.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The design of this verse and the following verses seems to be, to prove that Christians, by partaking of the Lord's Supper, are solemnly set apart to the service of the Lord Jesus; that they acknowledge Him as their Lord, and dedicate themselves to him.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: The cup of blessing—answering to the Jewish 'cup of blessing,' over which thanks were offered in the Passover. It was in doing so that Christ instituted this part of the Lord’s Supper.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: It was the point of transition between two economies and their two great festivals, the one about to close for ever, the other immediately to open and run its majestic career until from earth it be transferred to heaven.

question: What is eternal life?
short_answer: Eternal life, according to Jesus, isn't merely endless existence but a profound relationship with God and his Son, Jesus Christ – a life of knowing Him (John 17:3). This is provided by God through Jesus and secured in him.
expanded:
The concept of eternal life extends far beyond simple immortality. John 17:3 emphasizes that it’s “this is life eternal—that they might know thee, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom thou hast sent.” This knowledge isn't superficial; it involves a deep understanding and connection with God, leading to joy and transformation. JFB clarifies that this life is “a life of acquaintance with God in Christ,” signifying a vital relationship rather than mere existence.

Barnes highlights the benevolent love of God as foundational to eternal life, stating that God desires the happiness of all people even while hating sin. This love manifests through Jesus Christ, who provides the means for obtaining this eternal life (1 John 5:11).  Jesus’ claim in John 11:25, “I am the resurrection and the life,” underscores his power to overcome death and grant everlasting life to those who believe.

Ultimately, JFB explains that eternal life is secured 'in' Jesus Christ – it is essentially and operatively found within him. This means that through faith in Jesus, the temporary separation of soul and body is transcended, and believers experience a life that will never sink into death.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This is life eternal - This is the source of eternal life; or it is in this manner that it is to be obtained. The knowledge of God and of his Son Jesus Christ is itself a source of unspeakable and eternal joy.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: this is—that. life eternal, then, is not mere conscious and unending existence, but a life of acquaintance with God in Christ (Job 22:21).

question: Why does God allow suffering?
short_answer: God allows suffering as a means to ultimately bring about good for those who love Him, contributing to their salvation and character development.
expanded:
The Bible offers comfort and understanding regarding the presence of suffering in our lives. Romans 8:28 assures believers that "all things work together for good to them that love God," even amidst trials and afflictions. This isn't a promise of ease, but rather a guarantee that these hardships are part of a larger plan directed by an infinitely wise God.

James 1:2 encourages Christians to “count it all joy” when facing various temptations or trials. These experiences serve as tests and opportunities for purification, refining the Christian character. Suffering isn't presented as a punishment but as a catalyst for growth, fostering patience (Romans 5:3) and endurance.

Hebrews 12:10 highlights that God’s chastisement, though sometimes painful, is ultimately for our benefit, even if it seems brief compared to the eternal perspective. Unlike earthly parents who may correct based on their own limited understanding, God's discipline aims at a higher purpose and reflects His perfect wisdom.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: And we know - This verse introduces another source of consolation and support, drawn from the fact that all flyings are under the direction of an infinitely wise Being, who has purposed the salvation of the Christian, and who has so appointed all things that they shall contribute to it.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "We know that to them that love God" (compare 1Co 2:9; Eph 6:24; Jas 1:12; 2:5) "all things work together for good [even] to them who are the called (rather, 'who are called') according to His (eternal) purpose."

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Count it all joy - Regard it as a thing to rejoice in; a matter which should afford you happiness. You are not to consider it as a punishment, a curse, or a calamity, but as a fit subject of felicitation.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Every possible trial to test and purify the Christian character.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But we glory - The word used here is the same that is in Romans 5:2 , translated, "we rejoice" καυχώμεθα kauchōmetha. It should have been so rendered here.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Patience is the quiet endurance of what we cannot but wish removed...whether it be the withholding of promised good, or the continued experience of positive ill.

question: What is the kingdom of God?
short_answer: The kingdom of God refers to God's reign or rule, both internally in the hearts of believers and externally as his laws are obeyed and the gospel spreads throughout the world. It is a moral and spiritual reality that exists wherever people submit to God’s grace.
expanded:
The concept of the kingdom of God appears frequently in Jesus' teachings and carries significant theological weight. Barnes explains that when we pray “Thy kingdom come,” it signifies a desire for God’s reign, where his laws are followed and his glory is manifested globally. JFB elaborates further, defining it as 'that moral and spiritual kingdom which the God of grace is setting up in this fallen world,' comprised of those who willingly submit to His rule and with Jesus Christ as its head.

The kingdom isn't always visible or easily identifiable; Luke 17:21 highlights that the Messiah’s arrival wouldn’t be marked by outward pomp, but rather a silent and unassuming presence. This suggests an internal, spiritual dimension alongside any external manifestations.  It is 'within you,' meaning it exists in the hearts of believers who have accepted Christ.

The kingdom's progression is linked to God’s timing and purpose. Revelation 11:15 indicates that the sounding of the seventh trumpet signifies the completion of world judgments and the introduction of a state where God’s reign becomes fully established, marking the winding up of worldly affairs and the beginning of the saints' reign.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The time is fulfilled - That is, the time for the appearance of the Messiah, the time so long foretold, has come. 'The kingdom of God is at hand.'... The petition is the expression of a wish that God may 'reign' everywhere; that his laws may be obeyed; and especially that the gospel of Christ may be advanced everywhere, until the world shall be filled with his glory.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: The kingdom of God is that moral and spiritual kingdom which the God of grace is setting up in this fallen world, whose subjects consist of as many as have been brought into hearty subjection to His gracious scepter, and of which His Son Jesus is the glorious Head.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: shut up within this or that sharply defined and visible geographical or ecclesiastical limit. within you—is of an internal and spiritual character (as contrasted with their outside views of it).

question: What is the covenant?
short_answer: The covenant refers to a solemn agreement or promise between God and His people, initially established with Israel at Sinai but superseded by a 'new covenant' through Jesus Christ. This new covenant is characterized by spiritual understanding and lasting forever.
expanded:
The concept of the covenant is central to biblical theology, representing a binding agreement between God and humanity. Initially, God entered into a covenant with Israel at Mount Sinai, marked by material sanctions and laws – essentially establishing a framework for their relationship (Jeremiah 31:31). However, Jeremiah prophesied a 'new covenant' that would replace the old, characterized by spiritual understanding written on the heart and lasting eternally. 

According to Hebrews 8:6, Christ has obtained “a more excellent ministry” as the mediator of this new covenant, which surpasses the former in dignity and endurance. This new covenant is not merely for a select group but relates to God's promises and blessings (Hebrews 8:10-11). Luke 22:20 highlights a transition point between these two economies, marking the beginning of the new covenant.

The initial establishment of covenants, like that with Abraham in Genesis 15:18, involved symbolic rituals to signify the agreement and its implications. God’s willingness to enter into such agreements demonstrates His commitment to relationship and promises, even when involving a seemingly unequal power dynamic.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: God at Sinai made a covenant with His people, of which the sanctions were material, or (where spiritual) materially understood. Necessarily therefore the Mosaic Church was temporary, but the sanctions of Jeremiah's Church are spiritual - written in the heart - and therefore it must take the place of the former covenant.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: The new covenant is made with literal Israel and Judah, not with the spiritual Israel, that is, believers, except secondarily, and as grafted on the stock of Israel (Ro 11:16-27).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But now hath he obtained - That is, Christ. A more excellent ministry - A service of a higher order, or of a more exalted nature.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "The messenger (angel) of the covenant." which—Greek, "one which" [Alford]: inasmuch as being one which. established—Greek, "enacted as a law."

question: What are the gifts of the Spirit?
short_answer: The gifts of the Spirit are various endowments conferred on Christians by God's grace, designed for the edification of the church. These include apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors and teachers, as well as other abilities to contribute to the welfare of others.
expanded:
Paul addresses the topic of spiritual gifts in several letters, emphasizing that they are given by God’s grace and not earned. 1 Corinthians 12:4 highlights “diversities of gifts,” produced by the same Holy Spirit, while Romans 12:6 clarifies that all such endowments are regarded as gifts from God, preventing pride. Ephesians 4:11 further elaborates on these gifts, specifically mentioning apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors and teachers – ministers appointed for the extension and edification of the church.

These spiritual gifts aren't limited to formal ministry roles; 1 Peter 4:10 explains that “every man hath received a gift,” referring to any endowment by which we can do good to others. This encompasses various abilities, not just preaching the word, but all qualifications provided by religion to help others. The Spirit’s gifts are intended for the common good of the Christian community, with each believer acting as a steward for the edification of the whole.

The concept of spiritual gifts is intricately linked to the Holy Trinity; 1 Corinthians 12:5-6 notes that Christ and the Father are also involved in their distribution. These gifts are not self-appointed but bestowed by God, demonstrating His provision for the church’s growth and well-being.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Now there are diversities of gifts - There are different endowments conferred on Christians...The design for which he refers to these various endowments is evidently to show those whom he addressed, that since they are all produced by the same.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 4. diversities of gifts—that is, varieties of spiritual endowments peculiar to the several members of the Church: compare 'dividing to every man severally' (1Co 12:11). same Spirit—The Holy Trinity appears here: the Holy Spirit in this verse; Christ in 1Co 12:5; and the Father in 1Co 12:6.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Having then gifts - All the endowments which Christians have are regarded by the apostle as gifts. God has conferred them; and this fact, when properly felt, tends much to prevent our thinking of ourselves more highly than we ought to think.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 6-8. Having then gifts differing according to the grace given to us—Here, let it be observed, all the gifts of believers alike are viewed as communications of mere grace.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: And he gave some, apostles - He gave some to be apostles... And some, evangelists - see the notes on Acts 21:8 ; compare 2 Timothy 4:5 .

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 11. Greek, emphatical. 'Himself' by His supreme power...Ministers did not give themselves.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: As every man hath received the gift - The word rendered 'the gift' (χάρισμα charisma,) in the Greek, without the article, means 'endowment' of any kind, but especially that conferred by the Holy Spirit.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 'every—'even as each man hath received,' in whatever degree, and of whatever kind. The Spirit's gifts (literally, 'gift of grace,' that is, gratuitously bestowed) are the common property of the Christian community...

question: What is the fruit of the Spirit?
short_answer: The fruit of the Spirit refers to the virtues produced within believers by the Holy Spirit. These include love, joy, gentleness, faith, goodness, and faithfulness, demonstrating a life transformed by divine influence.
expanded:
According to Paul in Galatians 5:22, the “fruit of the Spirit” describes what the Holy Spirit produces in those who believe. It's not about performing works to earn spiritual standing; rather, it’s about allowing the Spirit to cultivate virtues within a person, distinguishing these qualities from actions stemming from human nature. These fruits are described as goodness, righteousness, and truth – characteristics that demonstrate one's identity as a child of light (Ephesians 5:9).

The commentaries highlight specific attributes encompassed by this fruit, including love, gentleness, faith, and faithfulness. JFB notes that love is the “leader of the band of graces,” while gentleness signifies a conciliatory nature. Faith, in this context, extends to trustfulness towards both God and humanity.

John 15:4 uses the analogy of a vine and branches to illustrate the importance of abiding in Christ for continued fruitfulness. Just as a branch cannot bear fruit apart from the vine, believers require a living connection with Christ to manifest the Spirit’s fruit. Romans 8:6 further emphasizes that pursuing carnal desires leads to condemnation, while being spiritually minded results in life and peace.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But the fruit of the Spirit - That which the Holy Spirit produces. It is not without design, evidently, that the apostle uses the word "Spirit" here, as denoting that these things do not flow from our own nature.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: love—the leader of the band of graces (1Co 13:1-13). gentleness—Greek, "benignity," conciliatory to others; whereas "goodness,” though ready to do good, has not such suavity of manner.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: For the fruit of the Spirit - That is, since the Holy Spirit through the gospel produces goodness, righteousness, and truth, see that you exhibit these in your lives, and thus show that you are the children of light.

question: What does it mean to be filled with the Spirit?
short_answer: To be filled with the Spirit means to be entirely under His influence and power, allowing Him to pervade all one's faculties. This results in speaking boldly, experiencing unity, and yielding to divine guidance rather than resisting it.
expanded:
The concept of being 'filled with the Spirit' is deeply connected to ecstatic experiences and divine empowerment. According to JFB, this phrase draws a parallel to the state of intoxication from wine, signifying an exhilarating influence – though contrasting it as a positive experience derived from God’s power rather than worldly indulgence. Barnes notes that it signifies “all the faculties are pervaded by [the Spirit], engaged in it, or under its influence.”

In Acts 2:4, those gathered at Pentecost were filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other languages, demonstrating a profound spiritual manifestation. This wasn't merely about receiving gifts but experiencing an overwhelming presence that compelled them to act boldly and share the message of God. Similarly, in Acts 4:31, after prayer, “the place was shaken,” signifying a divine response and further evidence of the Spirit’s power.

Galatians 5:16 emphasizes walking 'in the Spirit' as a means of overcoming sinful desires. Barnes explains this to mean living under the Holy Spirit’s influences, yielding to His suggestions rather than resisting them. JFB clarifies that it is not about eliminating evil lusts entirely but ensuring they are “not fulfilled” by allowing the Spirit to dominate one's actions and thoughts.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: To be filled with anything is a phrase denoting that all the faculties are pervaded by it, engaged in it, or under its influence... This is the true rule about overcoming the propensities of your carnal natures... live under the influences of the Holy Spirit; admit those influences fully into your hearts.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: The effect in inspiration was that the person was 'filled' with an ecstatic exhilaration, like that caused by wine... The spirit and the flesh mutually exclude one another. It is promised, not that we should have no evil lusts, but that we should 'not fulfil' them.

question: How does the Holy Spirit help us?
short_answer: The Holy Spirit helps us in several ways: he sustains us in our weaknesses through prayer, guides us into truth, teaches us all things, brings to remembrance what Jesus taught, and leads us as children of God.
expanded:
Romans 8:26 highlights that the Holy Spirit assists believers when they are weak. The commentary notes that this isn't merely about one specific weakness, but rather the general spiritual frailty inherent in our current state. We often struggle to know precisely what to pray for or how to articulate it effectively, and the Spirit intercedes on our behalf, bridging that gap.

The Holy Spirit also acts as a guide, leading believers into truth. John 16:13 states that when He comes, “he will show you things to come,” revealing truths about God’s future kingdom and the unfolding of His plan. This guidance isn't arbitrary; it is rooted in what the Spirit hears from God, much like Jesus received instruction from the Father.

Furthermore, John 14:26 promises that the Holy Spirit will teach believers all things and bring to remembrance what Jesus has said. This underscores the Spirit’s role in preserving and transmitting divine truth, ensuring that foundational teachings are not lost or forgotten. Romans 8:14 connects this guidance with sonship; those who allow themselves to be led by the Spirit demonstrate their identity as children of God.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Likewise the Spirit - This introduces a new source of consolation and support, what is derived from the Spirit. It is a full promise that they would be inspired, and that in organizing the church, and in recording the truths necessary for its edification, they would be under the direction of the Holy Spirit.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: But after the like manner doth the Spirit also help. our infirmity; not merely the one infirmity here specified, but the general weakness of the spiritual life in its present state...This arises partly from th

question: What is the conviction of the Holy Spirit?
short_answer: The conviction of the Holy Spirit is a process where individuals are persuaded by God's truth that they are sinners and in need of salvation. It involves a demonstration of sin through reasoning and evidence, leading to grief and a desire for change.
expanded:
The concept of conviction by the Holy Spirit begins with demonstrating truths about God and sin, as described in John 16:8 where it states He “will convince the world of sin.” This isn't merely accusation but rather presenting arguments that persuade individuals to recognize their wrongdoing. Barnes explains this as applying divine truths to people’s minds so they feel like sinners.

Acts 2:37 illustrates the effect of this conviction, with those hearing Peter’s message being “pricked in their heart.” JFB notes this is a demonstration of genuine compunction and docility, seeking correction after realizing past mistakes. This piercing grief signifies a profound awareness of sin and a desire for redemption.

The Spirit's witness also operates internally, as Romans 8:16 reveals. The Holy Spirit bears witness “with our spirit,” confirming that believers are children of God. JFB elaborates on this, describing it as the Holy Spirit setting His seal to the cry of ‘Abba, Father’ within us.

Finally, Hebrews 10:15 emphasizes that the Holy Spirit is a witness to the truth revealed in Scripture. Barnes clarifies that this refers to the Spirit's inspiration of the Scriptures themselves, providing further evidence and confirmation of God's plan for salvation.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: He will reprove - The word translated "reprove" means commonly to demonstrate by argument, to prove, to persuade anyone to do a thing by presenting reasons. It hence means also to convince of anything, and particularly to convince of crime.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: he will reprove—This is too weak a word to express what is meant. Reproof is indeed implied in the term employed, and doubtless the word begins with it. But convict or convince is the thing intended;

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: They were pricked in their heart - The word translated were "pricked," κατενύγησαν katenugēsan, is not used elsewhere in the New Testament. It properly denotes "to pierce or penetrate with a needle, lancet, or sharp instrument"; and then "to pierce with grief, or acute pain of any k

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: So Saul of Tarsus (Ac 9:6). Matthew Poole's Commentary They were pricked in their heart; so great and true their grief, they were concerned as if

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The Spirit - The Holy Spirit. That the Holy Spirit here is intended, is evident, (1) Because this is the natural meaning of the expression; (2) Because it is of the Holy Spirit that the apostle is mainly treating here;

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: The testimony of our own spirit is borne in that cry of conscious sonship, "Abba, Father"; but we are not therein alone; for the Holy Ghost within us, yea, even in that very cry which it is His to draw forth, sets His own distinct seal to ours;

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Whereof the Holy Ghost is a witness to us - That is, the Holy Spirit is a proof of the truth of the position here laid down - that the one atonement made by the Redeemer lays the foundation for the eternal perfection of all who are sanctified.

question: What does it mean to grieve the Holy Spirit?
short_answer: To grieve the Holy Spirit means to afflict or sadden Him through actions and behaviors inconsistent with Christian living, such as corrupt conversation or resisting His guidance.
expanded:
The concept of grieving the Holy Spirit refers to the ability of believers to cause sorrow to God's Holy Spirit through their choices and conduct. Barnes explains that the word used signifies 'to afflict with sorrow; to make sad.' This isn’t about angering God in a punitive sense, but rather causing emotional pain, similar to how we feel when someone ungrateful rejects our kindness – “for there is nothing which we more deeply feel than the ingratitude of those who have been benefited by us.”

The commentary from JFB emphasizes that this applies specifically to believers and arises from inconsistencies in their lives. These actions can include things like 'corrupt or worthless conversation.' It’s a recognition of the Spirit's tenderness and close relationship with believers, as illustrated by Isaiah’s depiction of Israel “vexing” His Holy Spirit.

Furthermore, resisting the guidance of the Holy Spirit, as seen in the example of the stiff-necked Jews who consistently opposed God’s will (Acts 7:51), is a significant way to grieve Him. This resistance demonstrates a rejection of divine truth and hinders spiritual growth, ultimately causing sorrow for the Spirit who desires our flourishing.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: And grieve not the Holy Spirit of God - This is addressed to Christians, and it proves that it is possible for them to grieve the Holy Spirit. The word used here - λυπεῖτε lupeite - means properly to afflict with sorrow; to make sad or sorrowful...for there is nothing which we more deeply feel than the ingratitude of those who have been benefited by us.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 30. grieve not—A condescension to human modes of thought most touching…This verse refers to believers, who grieve the Spirit by inconsistencies such as in the context are spoken of, corrupt or worthless conversation…

question: What is intercessory prayer?
short_answer: Intercessory prayer involves praying on behalf of others, seeking God's blessing and protection for them.
expanded:
The concept of intercessory prayer is evident throughout scripture. Paul’s instruction in 1 Timothy 2:1 emphasizes the importance of prayers “for all men,” indicating a responsibility to petition God on behalf of others, not just prescribed individuals or groups. This contrasts with a rigid liturgy and highlights the freedom to seek God's favor for everyone.

James 5:16 further illustrates this principle by encouraging confession of faults “one to another” which can be understood as seeking support and prayer from fellow believers in times of need, demonstrating a communal aspect of intercession.  The passage clarifies that such confessions are not directed towards priests, but rather within the Christian community.

The assurance found in Romans 8:34 reinforces the power of Christ’s intercession for believers; since He bore our condemnation, who then will condemn us? This highlights a divine intercessor acting on behalf of those who believe. John 17:9 specifically describes Jesus praying “for them,” distinguishing his disciples from “the world” and requesting God's protection for those chosen to belong to Him.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: I exhort, therefore - Margin, "desire." The word exhort, however, better expresses the sense of the original. The exhortation here is not addressed particularly to Timothy, but relates to all who were called to lead in public prayer; 1 Timothy 2:8 .

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: CHAPTER 2 1Ti 2:1-15. Public Worship. Direction as to Intercessions for All Men, since Christ Is a Ransom for All. The Duties of Men and Women Respectively in Respect to Public Prayer. Woman's Subjection; Her Sphere of Duty.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Confess your faults one to another - This seems primarily to refer to those who were sick, since it is added, "that ye may be healed."

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "The oldest authorities read, 'Confess, THEREFORE,' &c. Not only in the particular case of sickness, but universally confess.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Who is he that condemneth? - Who shall pass sentence of condemnation, and consign to perdition?

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: yea rather, that is risen again—to make good the purposes of His death.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: I pray for them - In view of their dangers and trials, he sought the protection and blessing of God on them.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 9-14. I pray for them—not as individuals merely, but as representatives of all such in every succeeding age (see on [1878]Joh 17:20).

question: Why does God answer prayer?
short_answer: God answers prayer because he desires a relationship with believers and aligns his responses with his will. Persistent, sincere requests made by those abiding in Christ's teachings are met with divine favor.
expanded:
The Bible presents multiple facets to understanding why God answers prayer. Matthew 7:7 encourages earnest seeking, stating, "Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you." This emphasizes the importance of perseverance and a sincere desire when approaching God in prayer.

John 15:7 further clarifies that abiding in Christ's words – his doctrine and commandments – is crucial for receiving answers. As Barnes notes, this promise had particular reference to the apostles but applies to all Christians who live by faith in him.  James 4:2 warns against selfish desires that lead to conflict; true prayer aligns with God’s will.

Ultimately, 1 John 5:14 highlights the confidence believers have in Christ regarding answered prayers. This assurance stems from knowing they possess eternal life and are praying according to His will – a state where their desires harmonize with God's purposes.  The commentary suggests that when we fully understand and submit to God’s will, it becomes impossible not to receive what is asked for.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: There are here three different forms presented of seeking the things which we need from God - asking, 'seeking, and knocking. The latter is taken from the act of knocking at a door for admittance...The promise is, that what we seek shall be given us. It is of course implied that we seek with a proper spirit, with humility, sincerity, and perseverance.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "But ah!” might some humble disciple say, 'I cannot persuade myself that I have any interest with God.' To meet this, our Lord gives the triple assurance of success to our believing efforts.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This promise had particular reference to the apostles. It is applicable to other Christians only so far as they are in circumstances similar to the apostles, and only so far as they possess their spirit.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: If we knew God's will thoroughly, and submitted to it heartily, it would be impossible for us to ask anything for the spirit or for the body which He should not perform; it is this ideal state which the apostle has in view.

question: What is the Lord's Prayer?
short_answer: The Lord's Prayer is a model prayer given by Jesus, designed to express the manner in which we should pray. It’s not necessarily meant to be recited verbatim, but rather to guide our prayers and reflect the substance of what we ask for.
expanded:
According to Matthew 6:9, the Lord's Prayer is a composition unequalled for its comprehensiveness and beauty, likely drawing from existing Jewish prayers. Jesus presented it as a “model” – not a rigid script – demonstrating how to approach God in prayer rather than dictating specific words. Luke’s account (Luke 11:2-4) presents a slightly different wording, further suggesting the flexibility of this prayer.

The prayer itself petitions for God's kingdom to come and His will to be done on earth as it is in heaven. It also asks for daily provision, acknowledging our dependence on God for sustenance. The petition uses plural language, indicating that it’s intended for use by a community or group of people.

While the exact wording may vary, the essence of the Lord's Prayer remains a powerful guide for believers seeking to communicate with God. Both Matthew and Luke acknowledge variations in the prayer's phrasing, emphasizing that the substance and spirit are more important than strict adherence to specific words.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This passage contains the Lord's prayer, a composition unequalled for comprehensiveness and for beauty. It is supposed that some of these petitions were taken from those in common use among the Jews... This prayer is given as a “model.” It is designed to express the “manner” in which we are to pray, evidently not the precise words or petitions which we are to use.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Did it consist only of hints or directions for prayer, it could only be used as a directory; but seeing it is an actual prayer—designed, indeed, to show how much real prayer could be compressed into the fewest words, but still, as a prayer, only the more incomparable for that—it is strange that there should be a doubt whether we ought to pray that very prayer.

question: Who is Jesus Christ?
short_answer: Jesus Christ is eternally existent, pre-dating creation itself. He is the image of the invisible God and the 'brightness of his glory,' existing from everlasting to everlasting.
expanded:
The New Testament reveals that Jesus Christ possesses an eternal existence, predating even Abraham. John 1:1 indicates that the “Word” – which would become incarnate as Jesus – existed “in the beginning,” before creation. This echoes Genesis 1:1 and signifies a timeless reality.

The apostle Paul in Colossians 1:15 describes Christ as "the image of the invisible God," emphasizing his perfect representation of divine nature and exaltation. He is also portrayed as the Creator and Sustainer of all things, demonstrating his role in both the natural and spiritual realms. Hebrews 1:3 further clarifies this by stating that Jesus is “the brightness of [God’s] glory,” an effulgence or radiance emanating from God.

In John 8:58, Jesus makes a profound declaration, "Before Abraham was, I am." This statement signifies His eternal being and existence outside the constraints of time. The Jews understood this as a claim to equality with God, prompting them to attempt to stone Him.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: In the beginning - This expression is used also in Genesis 1:1 . John evidently has allusion here to that place, and he means to apply to 'the Word' an expression which is there applied 'to God.'...The meaning is: that the 'Word' had an existence before the world was created.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Paul describes Him: (1) in relation to God and creation (Col 1:15-17); (2) in relation to the Church (Col 1:18-20). As the former regards Him as the Creator (Col 1:15, 16) and the Sustainer (Col 1:17) of the natural world; so the latter, as the source and stay of the new moral creation.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: First, in what manner does it refer to the Redeemer? To his divine nature? To the mode of his existence before he was incarnate? Or to him as he appeared on earth?

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "Light of (from) light” [Nicene Creed]. 'Who is so senseless as to doubt concerning the eternal being of the Son? For when has one seen light without effulgence?'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The expression I am, though in the present tense, is clearly designed to refer to a past time. Thus, in Psalm 90:2 , 'From everlasting to everlasting thou art God.' Applied to God, it denotes continued existence without respect to time...

question: What is the incarnation?
short_answer: The incarnation refers to God, in the form of Jesus Christ, taking on human flesh and becoming a man. This act involved a being of higher order than humanity assuming the appearance or nature of a man.
expanded:
The concept of the incarnation is central to Christian theology, referring specifically to the embodiment of God as Jesus Christ. John 1:14 describes this event, stating that "the Word was made flesh," signifying that the divine second person of the Trinity became human.
