This wasn't merely an appearance; it involved a true assumption of human nature.

The passage in Philippians 2:6 further illuminates the incarnation by discussing Jesus’s prior existence “in the form of God.”  This 'form' refers to the outward manifestation of divine characteristics and beauty, contrasting with his subsequent humbling into the 'form of a servant.' This highlights the remarkable act of self-abasement involved in becoming human.

The timing of this event was also significant. Galatians 4:4 speaks of “the fullness of the time” when Jesus came, indicating that God had meticulously planned and prepared for his arrival.  This wasn't a premature intervention; it occurred when humanity’s need for salvation was fully realized and all conditions were right for Christ's mission.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: And the Word was made flesh - The word "flesh," here, is evidently used to denote "human nature" or "man." See Matthew 16:17 ; Matthew 19:5 ; Matthew 24:22 ; Luke 3:6 ; Romans 1:3 ; Romans 9:5 . The "Word" was made "man."

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: was made flesh—BECAME MAN, in man's present frail, mortal condition, denoted by the word 'flesh' (Isa 40:6; 1Pe 1:24). It is directed probably against the Docetæ, who held that Christ was not really but only apparently man.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Who, being in the form of God - There is scarcely any passage in the New Testament which has given rise to more discussion than this. The importance of the passage on the question of the divinity of the Saviour will be perceived at once...

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: the form of God (the divine essence is not meant: but the external self-manifesting characteristics of God, the form shining forth from His glorious essence).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But when the fulness of the time was come - The full time appointed by the Father; the completion (filling up, πλήρωμα plērōma,) of the designated period for the coming of the Messiah...

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "the fulness of the time—namely, 'the time appointed by the Father' (Ga 4:2). God does nothing prematurely, but, foreseeing the end from the beginning, waits till all is ripe for the execution of His purpose.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: And, without controversy - Undeniably, certainly. The object of the apostle is to say that the truth which he was about to state admitted of no dispute.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: great is the mystery of godliness: (namely), He who (so the oldest manuscripts and versions read for 'God') was manifested in (the) flesh...

question: Why is Jesus the only way to God?
short_answer: Jesus is the only way to God because He acts as the mediator between God and humanity, offering reconciliation and access through His sacrifice. There is no other name given among men by which salvation can be obtained.
expanded:
The Bible clearly states that Jesus Christ is the sole path to God. John 14:6 declares, "I am the way…no man cometh unto the Father but by me," emphasizing His role as a guide and leader on the journey to divine connection. This isn't merely about following instructions or imitating an example; it’s about relying on His merits and being led by Him.

The concept of Jesus as the only mediator is further reinforced in Acts 4:12, which asserts that "there is no other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved." This highlights the uniqueness of Christ's position – He bridges the gap between a holy God and sinful humanity.  Furthermore, 1 Timothy 2:5 explains this exclusivity stems from the fact that there is one God and one mediator, underscoring His universal concern for all people.

 Hebrews 9:15 elaborates on Jesus’s mediatorial role, stating He provides an “effectual atonement” for sin.  His death initiated a new covenant, ensuring forgiveness and eternal inheritance for those called to salvation. Christ's sacrifice is the necessary condition for reconciliation, making Him the only viable route to God.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: I am the way - See Isaiah 35:8 . By this is meant, doubtless, that they and all others were to have access to God only by obeying the instructions, imitating the example, and depending on the merits of the Lord Jesus Christ. He was the leader in the road, the guide to the wandering, the teacher of the ignorant, and the example to all.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "THE Way" to the Father—"no man cometh unto the Father but by Me"; He is "THE Truth" of all we find in the Father when we get to Him…and He is all “THE Life” that shall ever flow to us and bless us from the Godhead thus approached and thus manifested in Him.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Neither is there salvation - The word 'salvation' properly denotes any 'preservation,' or keeping anything in a 'safe' state; a preserving from harm...This work refers primarily to a deliverance of the soul from sin.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: How sublimely does the apostle, in these closing words, shut up these rulers of Israel to Jesus for salvation, and in what universal and emphatic terms does he hold up his Lord as the one Hope of men!

question: What is the ascension of Christ?
short_answer: The ascension of Christ refers to his departure from Earth after his resurrection, witnessed by his apostles. It signifies his return to heaven and exaltation as Lord, securing gifts for believers and establishing him as our great high priest.
expanded:
The ascension of Christ is a pivotal event in Christian theology, marking the culmination of his earthly ministry. According to Acts 1:9, Jesus was “taken up” into heaven while his apostles were watching, disappearing into a cloud. This wasn't simply an assumption; it was a visible and public departure, ensuring its authenticity as opposed to merely assuming he had risen from the dead.

Paul connects Christ’s ascension with God’s bestowal of spiritual gifts upon humanity, referencing Psalm 68:18 in Ephesians 4:8.  The Psalm originally described David bringing the Ark of the Covenant into Zion, and Paul applies this imagery to Christ's ascent, portraying him as the one who brings blessings and gifts to his people. This signifies that Christ’s ascension wasn’t just a physical departure but also an act of divine authority.

The significance is further underscored in Hebrews 4:14, which describes Jesus as having “passed into the heavens,” signifying his entry into God's presence.  This highlights his role as our great high priest, surpassing the Levitical priesthood and providing access to God for believers. Romans 8:34 emphasizes that Christ, who died for us, will not condemn those who are elect, demonstrating the security and grace afforded through his ascension.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: It is not affirmed in the New Testament that they 'saw him rise' from the dead, because the evidence of that fact could be better established by their seeing him after he was risen. But the truth of his 'ascension to heaven' could not be confirmed in that manner.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: while they beheld, he was taken up—See on [1932]Lu 24:50-53. Lest it should be thought He had disappeared when they were looking in some other direction, and so was only concluded to have gone up to heaven, it is here expressly said that 'while they were looking He was taken up, and a cloud received Him out of their sight.'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The 'point' of the argument here is, that Christ, when he ascended to heaven, obtained certain 'gifts' for people, and that those gifts are bestowed upon his people in accordance with this.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Paul quotes it of Christ ascending to heaven, who is therefore God. captivity—that is, a band of captives.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The 'object' is to show that Christians have a great High Priest as really as the Jews had; to show wherein he surpassed the Levitical priesthood; to show how all that was said of the Aaronic priesthood, and all the types pertaining to that priesthood, were fulfilled in the Lord Jesus.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: passed into the heavens—rather, 'passed through the heavens,' namely, those which come between us and God, the aerial heaven, and that above the latter containing the heavenly bodies, the sun, moon, &c.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: It is Christ that died - Or as it may be rendered, 'Shall Christ who has died, condemn them?' The argument here is, that as Christ died to save them, and not to destroy them, he will not condemn them.

question: What is the second coming of Christ?
short_answer: The second coming of Christ refers to His visible return to Earth in glory and power. It will be accompanied by a shout, the voice of an archangel, and signs throughout the heavens, ultimately bringing final judgment and establishing His kingdom.
expanded:
According to scripture, the second coming of Christ is a future event where Jesus will descend from heaven in all his majesty (1 Thessalonians 4:16). This return won't be subtle; it will be marked by a “shout” – not necessarily made *by* Christ, but accompanying Him—and the voice of an archangel (JFB), signifying a triumphant and victorious King leading heavenly hosts. The imagery suggests a final confrontation with sin, death, and Satan (JFB). 

While Jesus spoke of signs preceding his coming (Matthew 24:30), these also relate to the destruction of Jerusalem, demonstrating that His words often have dual applications.  The disciples initially gazed expectantly into heaven after Christ's ascension (Acts 1:11), but were rebuked and reminded that He would return “in like manner” as they had seen Him go – a visible and personal reappearance.

The book of Revelation further illustrates this event with the vision of a white horse, upon which sits one called Faithful and True (Revelation 19:11), symbolizing ultimate victory for the church. This signifies not only judgment but also the establishment of Christ's eternal reign.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: For the Lord himself shall descend from heaven - notes, Acts 1:11 . With a shout - The word here used (κέλευσμα keleusma), does not elsewhere occur in the New Testament. It properly means a "cry' of excitement, or of arging on; an outcry, clamor, or shout..."

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 'as ye have seen Him go, in the like manner shall He come’—as personally, as visibly, as gloriously'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The disciples had asked him Matthew 24:3 what should be the sign of his coming, and "of the end of the world." In his answer he has reference to both events, and his language may be regarded as descriptive of both.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: And I saw heaven opened - He saw a new vision, as if an opening were made through the sky, and he was permitted to look into heaven. See the notes on Revelation 4:1 .

question: What is regeneration?
short_answer: Regeneration is a spiritual rebirth that fundamentally alters a person's inner being, enabling them to understand and accept divine truth. It’s not achieved through good works but is a gift of God's grace, initiated by the incorruptible word of God.
expanded:
The concept of regeneration is central to salvation, as illustrated in John 3:3 where Jesus explains that one must be “born again” to enter the kingdom of God. Nicodemus’s initial understanding was flawed, and Jesus emphasized that this rebirth isn't a physical process but a spiritual revolution within a person. This transformation involves a complete change in perspective and ability to grasp divine truths.

This regeneration is not earned through human effort or “works of righteousness” (Titus 3:5). Barnes clarifies that salvation doesn’t stem from our good deeds, as such actions would render the work of Christ unnecessary. Instead, it arises from God's mercy and grace, facilitated by His word. As JFB notes, there was an utter absence of “righteousness” within humanity before this divine intervention.

1 Peter 1:23 further illuminates regeneration as being born “of incorruptible seed, the abiding Word of God.” This signifies that spiritual rebirth is initiated and sustained by a power beyond human capacity. Ezekiel 36:26 describes it as receiving a “new heart” and “new spirit,” replacing a “stony heart” with one that is receptive to God’s will.  This new heart represents a change in motive, principle, and the ability to respond positively to divine guidance.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: It may seem remarkable that he should introduce this subject in this manner; but it should be remembered that Nicodemus acknowledged that he was a teacher come from God; that he implied by that his readiness and desire to receive instruction.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Nicodemus probably thought he had gone a long way, and expected, perhaps, to be complimented on his candor. Instead of this, he is virtually told that he has raised a question which he is not in a capacity to solve.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: It is a great and fundamental principle of the gospel that the good works of men come in for no share in the justification of the soul.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "We neither did works of righteousness, nor were saved in consequence of them; but His goodness did the whole" [Theophylact].

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Being born again - See the notes at John 3:3 . Not of corruptible seed - 'Not by virtue of any descent from human parents' - Doddridge.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: As natural relationship gives rise to natural affection, so spiritual relationship gives rise to spiritual, and therefore abiding love, even as the seed from which it springs is abiding, not transitory as earthly things.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Ezekiel the priest has in view the purifying rites prescribed by the Law, the symbolic purport of which is exhibited in Hebrews 9:13-14 ; Hebrews 10:22 .

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: new heart—mind and will. spirit—motive and principle of action. stony heart—unimpressible in serious things; like the 'stony ground' (Mt 13:5, 20), unfit for receiving the good seed so as to bring forth fruit.

question: Can a believer lose their salvation?
short_answer: The Bible teaches that believers have eternal life and cannot lose their salvation; however, there is a warning against falling away after experiencing God's grace, which makes renewal to repentance impossible.
expanded:
The question of whether a believer can lose their salvation is complex, with scriptural passages offering both assurance and warnings. John 10:28 states that Jesus gives believers “eternal life,” which implies a permanence not subject to destruction or punishment. This concept is further emphasized by Romans 8:38, where Paul expresses unwavering confidence that neither death nor life can separate believers from God’s love.

Philippians 1:6 speaks of God beginning a 'good work' in believers and assuring its completion, reinforcing the idea of a secure salvation. However, Hebrews 6:4 introduces a cautionary note, stating that it is “impossible to renew” those who have fallen away after experiencing enlightenment. This passage has been interpreted as referring to individuals who may have initially experienced religious feelings but were never truly saved, or perhaps as a warning against the seriousness of apostasy.

The commentators suggest that while God's gift of eternal life is secure, vigilance and perseverance in faith are essential for believers to remain steadfast and avoid falling into practices that would lead to spiritual ruin. The ultimate assurance rests on God’s faithfulness and promises.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: For I am persuaded - I have a strong and unwavering confidence. Latin Vulgate, "I am certain."

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: It is a very grand utterance, couched in the language of majestic authority.

question: How should we forgive others?
short_answer: We should forgive others as often as they seek forgiveness, without setting a limit on the number of times. Forgiveness involves forbearance, kindness, and putting aside any complaints or causes for blame we may have against them.
expanded:
The Bible emphasizes limitless forgiveness towards those who offend us. Jesus responded to Peter's question about forgiving seven times by stating, "Until seventy times seven,” indicating that there is no fixed number of times we should forgive (Matthew 18:22). This echoes a similar teaching in Luke 17:3-4, highlighting the importance of repeatedly offering forgiveness when it is sought. 

Furthermore, forgiveness isn't just about overlooking an offense; it requires forbearance and kindness towards others. Colossians 3:13 instructs us to “forbearing one another” and “forgiving one another,” demonstrating a willingness to suspend judgment and act with gentleness even when we have cause for complaint (JFB). Ephesians 4:32 expands on this, calling us to be kind and courteous towards each other, reflecting God’s own kindness and forgiveness shown to us in Christ.

Ultimately, the Bible connects our ability to forgive others to God's forgiveness of us. As JFB notes, “God hath shown Himself ‘kind, tender-hearted, and forgiving to you’; it is but just that you in turn shall be so to your fellow men.” This underscores the reciprocal nature of Christian love and the importance of extending grace as we have received it.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: It is, indeed, his duty to ask forgiveness, Luke 17:4 . If he does this, it is our duty to declare that we for...

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Jesus saith unto him, I say not unto thee, Until seven times; but, Until seventy times seven—that is, so long as it shall be needed and sought: you are never to come to the point of refusing forgiveness sincerely asked.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The idea is, that if another one has given us just occasion of complaint, we are to forgive him; that is, we are: (1) to harbor no malice against him; (2) we are to be ready to do him good as if he had not given us occasion of complain

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: even as—God hath shown Himself 'kind, tender-hearted, and forgiving to you’; it is but just that you in turn shall be so to your fellow men, who have not erred against you in the degree that you have erred against God (Mt 18:33).

question: What is spiritual warfare?
short_answer: Spiritual warfare refers to the Christian’s ongoing struggle against unseen spiritual forces of evil, not physical opponents. This battle is waged through prayer, faith, and reliance on God's power, utilizing 'weapons mighty through God' rather than earthly means.
expanded:
The concept of spiritual warfare is presented as a reality for believers, involving a constant “wrestling” or struggle (Ephesians 6:12). This isn’t a conflict with human beings (“not against flesh and blood”), but rather a battle against Satan and his demonic forces who are described as “principalities…the powers.” JFB emphasizes that successful wrestling with Satan requires intense prayer, mirroring Jacob's encounter with God (Genesis 32:24-29). 

The tools for this warfare are not carnal or earthly (2 Corinthians 10:4), but rather 'mighty through God.' These weapons include boldness of speech, ecclesiastical discipline, the power of the word, and spiritual gifts. Barnes clarifies that these aren’t reliant on human strength or talent, but derive their power solely from God.

Peter urges believers to be “sober” and “vigilant” (1 Peter 5:8), cautioning against complacency and reminding them to watch against the devil's schemes. James further instructs Christians to submit to God and resist the devil, promising that he will flee (James 4:7). This submission involves yielding to God’s arrangements for salvation and actively opposing temptation through faith and humble prayer.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: For we wrestle - Greek, "The wrestling to us;" or, "There is not to us a wrestling with flesh and blood." There is undoubtedly here an allusion to the ancient games of Greece, a part of the exercises in which consisted in wrestling; see the notes on 1 Corinthians 9:25-27 .

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Flesh and blood foes are Satan's mere tools, the real foe lurking behind them is Satan himself, with whom our conflict is.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But mighty through God - Not such as derive advantage from any power inherent in themselves. Their strength is derived from God alone.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "Be sober … vigilant—"Care," that is, anxiety, will intoxicate the soul; therefore be sober, that is, self-restrained.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Submit yourselves therefore to God - That is, in his arrangements for obtaining his favor. Yield to what he has judged necessary for your welfare in the life that is, and your salvation in the life to come.

question: What does it mean to bear your cross?
short_answer: To bear your cross means to deny yourself and be willing to renounce all, even laying down your life if required, following Christ’s example of suffering. It signifies a death to one's old self and desires, living instead through Christ.
expanded:
The concept of “bearing your cross” originates from Jesus’ teachings on discipleship, as recorded in the Gospels. He instructed his followers that if they wished to follow him, they must “deny themselves, and take up their cross, and follow me” (Matthew 16:24). This wasn't a literal instruction to carry an instrument of torture, but rather a metaphorical call to embrace hardship, sacrifice, and suffering for the sake of Christ.

The phrase is closely linked to the idea of putting off the “old man,” which refers to one’s former sinful nature and way of life (Romans 6:6). Paul elaborates on this in Galatians 2:20, stating, "I am crucified with Christ," signifying a symbolic death to his old self and a new life lived through Christ. This involves relinquishing personal desires and ambitions and aligning oneself with God’s will, even when it is difficult or painful.

Ultimately, bearing your cross represents a complete surrender to Christ's purpose, embracing the challenges and trials that come with following him. It is not about seeking suffering for its own sake but willingly accepting whatever burdens are placed upon you as part of living a life devoted to God.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This discourse is also recorded in Mark 8:34-38 ; Mark 9:1 ; and Luke 9:23-27 . Let him, deny himself - That is, let him surrender to God his will, his affections, his body, and his soul. Take up his cross - See the notes at Matthew 10:38 .

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: If any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me.

question: How should we love our neighbor?
short_answer: Loving your neighbor means fulfilling the law, specifically the second table which concerns our conduct towards others. This is prompted by God's love for us and demonstrates that we have His Spirit.
expanded:
The core of loving your neighbor is encapsulated in the principle that it resembles the importance and purity of loving God. Jesus emphasized this connection, stating that loving one’s neighbor is “like unto” Leviticus 19:18, highlighting its significance within the broader framework of divine law. This isn't merely about avoiding negative actions like adultery or theft; rather, it involves actively seeking our neighbor's good and fulfilling all duties owed to them.

The Apostle Paul further clarifies that love is the very essence of fulfilling the law, summarizing numerous commandments as being encompassed within this principle. He states that “this” – meaning loving one’s neighbor – regulates our conduct and reflects the nearness of salvation.

Furthermore, 1 John emphasizes that God's profound love for us serves as a powerful motivation to extend that same love to others.  As believers, we are called to reflect God's character by demonstrating love towards those around us, recognizing them as fellow children of God and representatives of the unseen divine.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The second is like unto it - Leviticus 19:18 . That is, it resembles it in importance, dignity, purity, and usefulness.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: For the [commandments], Thou shalt not kill, Thou shalt not commit adultery, Thou shalt not steal, Thou shalt not covet, and whatever other commandment [there may be], it is summed up”

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This law of love would prompt us to seek our neighbor's good; 'that' fact, that our salvation is near, would prompt us to be active and faithful in the discharge of all the duties we owe to him.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Beloved, if God so loved us, we ought also to love one another - (1) Because he is so much exalted above us, and if he has loved those who were so inferior and so unworthy, we ought to love those who are on a level with us; (2) because it is only in this way that we can show that we have his Spirit.

question: Why do Christians suffer?
short_answer: Christians suffer because they are heirs of God and joint-heirs with Christ, sharing in His sufferings to ultimately share in His glory. These trials serve as fiery tests, removing impurities and developing patience for a 'perfect work' within the soul.
expanded:
The concept of Christian suffering is deeply intertwined with our inheritance as children of God. As heirs, we are treated as sons and daughters, destined to partake in God’s favor both now and eternally (Romans 8:17). This inheritance isn't free from hardship; rather, it necessitates conformity to Christ, who Himself endured suffering (JFB, Romans 8:17).  We become 'joint-heirs with Christ,' experiencing trials that mirror His own. 

Peter emphasizes that Christians should not be surprised by fiery trials, comparing them to the refining of metals—a process designed to remove impurities and reveal true worth (Barnes, 1 Peter 4:12; JFB, 1 Peter 4:12). These trials are not random occurrences but rather have a purposeful design from God.  Paul's desire to 'know Christ' also includes understanding and experiencing the power of His resurrection, which involves facing adversity and embracing hope (Barnes, Philippians 3:10; JFB, Philippians 3:10).

Ultimately, suffering serves as an opportunity for patience to have its ‘perfect work’ within us. This means allowing trials to shape our character without resistance or complaint, leading to spiritual maturity and wholeness (Barnes, James 1:4; JFB, James 1:4). It is through enduring these challenges with joy that we are fully developed as Christians.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Beloved, think it not strange - Do not consider it as anything which you had no reason to expect; as anything which may not happen to others also. Concerning the fiery trial which is to try you - Referring, doubtless, to some severe persecution which was then impending.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 17. And if children, then heirs—'heirs also.' heirs of God—of our Father's kingdom. and joint-heirs with Christ—as the 'First-born among many brethren' (Ro 8:29), and as 'Heir of all things' (Heb 1:2).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But let patience have her perfect work - Let it be fairly developed; let it produce its appropriate effects without being hindered.

question: What is the role of a pastor?
short_answer: The pastor’s role, as described in scripture, involves tending to the flock of God through discipline and doctrine, much like a shepherd. They are responsible for overseeing the church, guarding against dangers, and maintaining their own piety, all with the understanding that this office is a divine gift.
expanded:
Ephesians 4:11 highlights that Christ gave some individuals as pastors and teachers to equip the saints for ministry and build up the body of Christ. These roles are distinct but interconnected, emphasizing the importance of spiritual growth and development within the church community. JFB notes that while apostles, prophets, and evangelists were extraordinary ministers, pastors and teachers represent the ordinary, ongoing care for a particular flock.

1 Peter 5:2 further clarifies the pastor’s duties, instructing elders to “feed the flock of God” through discipline and doctrine, leading, tending, and providing oversight. This echoes Christ's own instructions to Peter, emphasizing the pastoral responsibility to nurture and guide those entrusted to their care. The term 'elder' signifies dignity, while 'bishop' denotes the duty of overseeing.

Acts 20:28 underscores that pastors must first take heed to themselves, maintaining their personal piety and guarding against temptations unique to their office. This self-care is essential for effective ministry, as a minister’s own spiritual well-being directly impacts their ability to guide others. Paul also emphasizes the responsibility of overseeing the flock which the Holy Ghost has placed under their care.

Finally, 1 Timothy 3:1 establishes that those who desire the office of pastor should be considered carefully, recognizing that such a calling often arises from an irrepressible desire to preach the gospel and serve God. The phrase 'faithful is the saying' emphasizes the importance of this role and the need for discernment in selecting individuals fit for such responsibility.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: And he gave some, apostles - He gave some to be apostles. The "object" here is to show that he has made ample provision for the extension and edification of his church On the meaning of the word "apostles," and on their appointment by the Saviour, see the notes on Matthew 10:1 .

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: As the apostles, prophets, and evangelists were special and extraordinary ministers, so "pastors and teachers" are the ordinary stated ministers of a particular flock, including, probably, the bishops, presbyters, and deacons.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Feed the flock of God - Discharge the duties of a shepherd toward the flock. On the word "feed," see the notes at John 21:15 . It is a word which Peter would be likely to remember, from the solemn manner in which the injunction to perform the duty was laid on him by the Saviour.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Feed—Greek, "Tend as a shepherd," by discipline and doctrine. Lead, feed, heed: by prayer, exhortation, government, and example. The dignity is marked by the term "elder"; the duties of the office, to tend or oversee, by "bishop."

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Take heed, therefore - Attend to; be on your guard against the dangers which beset you, and seek to discharge your duty with fidelity. Unto yourselves - To your own piety, opinions, and mode of life.

question: Why should I go to church?
short_answer: Going to church is vital for Christian fellowship and spiritual growth. Hebrews 10:25 encourages believers not to forsake assembling together, as it fosters love and provokes good works.
expanded:
The Bible emphasizes the importance of gathering with fellow Christians. Hebrews 10:25 directly addresses this, urging believers “not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together.” This isn't merely about attending a building; it’s about participating in public worship and fellowship – a practice that strengthens faith and provides opportunities for mutual encouragement.

The early church exemplified this commitment. Acts 2:42 describes how they "continued steadfastly" in the apostles' doctrine, fellowship, breaking of bread, and prayer. This consistent gathering was essential for their spiritual consolidation and growth, as it provided instruction and fostered a sense of community.

Furthermore, Colossians 3:16 highlights that allowing “the word of Christ” to dwell richly within believers is often expressed through psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs – activities best experienced in a communal setting.  The church, as described in Ephesians 4:16, functions like a body with interconnected parts, each relying on the others for strength and vitality; regular participation contributes to this overall health.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together - That is, for purposes of public worship. Some expositors have understood the word rendered here as "assembling" - ἐπισυναγωγὴν episunagōgēn - as meaning "the society of Christians," or the church; and they have supposed that the object of the apostle here is, to exhort them. not to apostatize from the church.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: assembling of ourselves together—The Greek, "episunagoge," is only found here and 2Th 2:1 (the gathering together of the elect to Christ at His coming, Mt 24:31). The assembling or gathering of ourselves for Christian communion in private and public, is an earnest of our being gathered together to Him at His appearing.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: And they continued stedfastly - They persevered in, or they adhered to. This is the inspired record of the result. That any of these apostatized is nowhere recorded, and is not to be presumed.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: continued steadfastly in—"attended constantly upon." the apostles' doctrine—"teaching"; giving themselves up to the instructions which, in their raw state, would be indispensable to the consolidation of the immense multitude suddenly admitted to visible discipleship.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Let the word of Christ - The doctrine of Christ. Dwell in you richly in all wisdom - Abundantly, producing the spirit of true wisdom. That doctrine is adapted to make you wise.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Let the word of Christ—the Gospel word by which ye have been called. richly—(Col 2:2; Ro 15:14). in all wisdom—Alford joins this clause with "teaching," &c., not with "dwell in you," as English Version, for so we find in Col 1:28, "teaching in all wisdom".

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The church, compared with the human body. The idea is, that as the head in the human frame conveys vital influence, rigor, motion, etc., to every part of the body; so Christ is the source of life, and rigor, and energy, and increase to the church.

question: What is worship?
short_answer: Worship is approaching God with a spiritual attitude and offering praise to Him continually through Jesus Christ.
expanded:
The Bible reveals that true worship isn't about physical rituals or offerings, but rather an inward devotion. John 4:24 emphasizes that God is a spirit, meaning He cannot be pleased by material sacrifices or temple structures; instead, He desires those who worship Him “in spirit and in truth.” This highlights the importance of a sincere heart and genuine connection with God.

Romans 12:1 calls believers to present themselves as living sacrifices, dedicating their lives to God's service.  This dedication is rooted in gratitude for His mercies and signifies a complete surrender to His will. JFB notes that this involves “spiritual service proportioned to his being,” moving beyond mere outward actions.

Psalm 95:6 further illustrates the act of worship as an invitation to approach God with reverence, bowing down and inclining oneself in homage. Hebrews 13:13 reinforces this concept by stating we should offer “the sacrifice of praise” through Jesus Christ, continually expressing gratitude for salvation – a practice that transcends specific times or rituals.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: God is A spirit - This is the second reason why men should worship him in spirit and in truth. By this is meant that God is without a body; that he is not material or composed of parts; that he is invisible, in every place, pure and holy.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: God is not a corporeal being, made up of blood, and flesh, and bones, having senses as bodies have, to be pleased with sensible things; but he is a spiritual Being, the Father of spirits, and requireth a spiritual service proportioned to his being.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The apostle therefore calls on those to whom he wrote to dedicate themselves without reserve unto God.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: the all-comprehensive duty...by the mercies of God—those mercies, whose free and unmerited nature, glorious Channel, and saving fruits have been opened up at such length. that ye present…yield.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The word rendered 'worship,' means properly to bow down; to incline oneself; and then, to bow or prostrate oneself before anyone in order to do him homage, or reverence.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: come—or, “enter,” with solemn forms, as well as hearts.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: let us persevere in offering through him praise to God. That is, let us persevere in adherence to our religion.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: By him—as the Mediator of our prayers and praises (Joh 14:13, 14); not by Jewish observances (Ps 50:14, 23; 69:30, 31; 107:22; 116:17).

question: What is the law of Moses?
short_answer: The Law of Moses refers to the rules and regulations prescribed in the Old Testament, intended to guide people's conduct. It served as a 'schoolmaster' leading individuals to recognize their sinfulness and ultimately point towards faith in Christ.
expanded:
The phrase "Law of Moses" encompasses a broad range of rules and instructions given by God through Moses in the Old Testament. Barnes explains that it signifies “rules or laws by which the Jews and Gentiles pretended to frame their lives.” It wasn't simply about following commands, but striving for righteousness according to those prescribed standards.

The commentary highlights that the Law’s primary function was not to provide justification. Rather, as JFB notes, “by the law is the knowledge of sin,” revealing humanity’s shortcomings and need for redemption.  It served as a guide, or 'schoolmaster' (Galatians 3:24), leading people to recognize their dependence on God.

Jesus clarified that He did not come to abolish the Law but to fulfill it (Matthew 5:17). This fulfillment meant demonstrating its true purpose and revealing how faith in Christ transcends the requirements of the Law, ultimately offering a path to righteousness through grace. Romans 10:4 emphasizes that “Christ is the end of the law for righteousness,” signifying that He embodies and completes what the Law aimed to achieve.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The word 'Law' has, in the Scriptures, a great variety of significations. Its strict and proper meaning is, a rule of conduct prescribed by superior authority.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "So that the law hath been...our schoolmaster (or 'tutor'...to guide us unto Christ,' with whom we are no longer 'shut up' in bondage, but are freemen."

question: What is sacrifice in the Old Testament?
short_answer: In the Old Testament, sacrifice was a system ordained by God where animals were offered on the altar, with their blood representing atonement for sins. The practice stemmed from the understanding that 'the soul of the flesh is in the blood,' and it served as a temporary covering until a more perfect offering would come.
expanded:
The Old Testament sacrificial system was deeply intertwined with the concept of atonement and purification. According to Leviticus 17:11, God ordained sacrifices because “the life of the flesh is in the blood,” signifying that blood held immense spiritual significance. The act of shedding blood on the altar served as a means of making atonement for souls, though it was recognized as an imperfect solution – a 'shadow' of what was to come (Hebrews 10:1). 

The system wasn’t universal; some things were purified by fire or water. However, Barnes notes that “almost everything in the tabernacle and temple service…was consecrated or purified by blood.” This highlights its central role in maintaining ritual purity and seeking forgiveness for sins.

Isaiah 53:10 speaks of how it 'pleased the Lord' that the Messiah would suffer, fulfilling a divine plan. Ultimately, Hebrews 9:22 emphasizes that “without shedding of blood is no remission,” foreshadowing the ultimate sacrifice of Jesus Christ, who would fulfill the Old Testament system and provide true forgiveness.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The words rendered 'the very image' refer to a painting or statue which is finished, where every part...for it is not yet complete.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Instead of the daily ministry of the Levitical priests, Christ's service is perfected by the one sacrifice, whence He now sits on the right hand of God as a Priest-King, until all His foes shall be subdued unto Him.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: 'he was subjected to these sufferings, not on account of any sins of his, but because, under the circumstances of the case, his sufferings would be pleasing to Yahweh.'

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: God, as the sovereign author and proprietor of nature, reserved the blood to Himself and allowed men only one use of it—in the way of sacrifices.

question: What is prophecy?
short_answer: Prophecy, according to these texts, isn’t dictated by human will but originates from God. It involves declaring future events and God's purposes, serving as a revelation of His secret counsel to prophets.
expanded:
The concept of prophecy is presented in these passages as something divinely inspired rather than originating from human desire. 2 Peter 1:21 emphasizes that “the prophecy came not in old time…by the will of man,” highlighting its supernatural origin and connection to God's revelation, particularly within the Old Testament. The prophets served as messengers, interpreting God’s intentions and revealing His plans, whether concerning future events or broader teachings.

Isaiah 46:10 further illustrates prophecy through God's ability to “declaring the end from the beginning,” demonstrating a knowledge of future events that proves His divine nature. This capacity to foretell accurately reveals a pre-established purpose and plan for human affairs, which cannot be thwarted by any other power.

The process involves God revealing His 'secret counsel' to prophets (Amos 3:7), allowing them to communicate His will and warnings to the people. Deuteronomy 18:22 outlines a method of verifying a prophet’s authenticity through the fulfillment or failure of their predictions, although it cautions against solely relying on miraculous signs as proof.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: For the prophecy came not in old time - Margin, or, "at any." The Greek word (ποτὲ pote) will bear either construction. It would be true in either sense, but the reference is particularly to the recorded prophecies in the Old Testament...that it is not by the will of man.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 21. came not in old time—rather, "was never at any time borne" (to us). by the will of man…holy—One oldest manuscript has, “men FROM God”: the emissaries from God.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Declaring the end from the beginning - Foretelling accurately the course of future events. This is an argument to which God often appeals in proof that he is the only true God…

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: do all my pleasure—(Isa 53:10; Ro 9:19). Declaring the end from the beginning; foretelling from the beginning of the world, or from the beginning of your nation, those future events which should happen in succeeding ages…

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Surely the Lord God will do - For the Lord God "doeth" Nothing, but He revealeth His secret unto His servants the prophets - So our Lord saith, “And now I have told you before it come to pass, that, when it is come to pass, ye may believe” ( John 14:29 ; compare John 13:19 ).

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: his secret—namely, His purpose hidden from all, until it is revealed to His prophets (compare Ge 18:17).

question: How does the Old Testament point to Jesus?
short_answer: Jesus explained to his disciples that the Old Testament scriptures foreshadowed him. He stated he came not to abolish the law and the prophets but to fulfill them, revealing himself as the central figure whose coming was prophesied in those writings.
expanded:
After Jesus' resurrection, he appeared to his disciples and opened their minds to understand how the Old Testament pointed to him. Luke 24:44 states that Jesus explained to them “these are the words of the law of Moses, and the prophets, and the psalms” referring to the divisions within the Hebrew scriptures. He clarified that these writings contained prophecies about his death and resurrection.

The New Testament authors consistently emphasized this connection. In John 5:39, Jesus urged people to “search the scriptures,” asserting that they would find testimony concerning him. Matthew 5:17 highlights Jesus’ intention not to abolish the Old Testament but to fulfill it, demonstrating how his actions and teachings aligned with its principles.

The author of Hebrews further underscored this point by stating that God had spoken in various ways and at different times in the past, but “in these last days he has spoken to us in his Son” (Hebrews 1:1). This signifies a culmination of Old Testament prophecies and promises fulfilled in Jesus Christ. The Old Testament serves as a foundational witness to the coming of the Messiah.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: These are the words - Or this is the “fulfillment” of what I before told you respecting my death. See Luke 18:33 ; Mark 10:33 . While I was yet with you - Before my death.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 44-49. These are the words, &c.—that is, 'Now you will understand what seemed so dark to you when I told you about the Son of man being put to death and rising again' (Lu 18:31-34).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Search the scriptures - The word translated 'search' here means to 'search diligently' or 'search anxiously.'

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 'In the Scriptures ye find your charter of eternal life; go search them then, and you will find that I am the Great Burden of their testimony.'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Our Saviour was just entering on his work. It was important for him to state what he came to do...He therefore told them that he did not come for that end, but really to fulfill or accomplish what was in the law and the prophets.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 17. Think not that I am come—that I came. to destroy the law, or the prophets—that is, 'the authority and principles of the Old Testament.'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: God who at sundry times - The commencement of this Epistle varies from all the others which Paul wrote...This announcement was of particular importance.

question: What is the significance of the Psalms?
short_answer: The Psalms are a collection of sacred hymns and poems expressing praise, lament, thanksgiving, and trust in God. They serve as a guide for understanding God's will and finding comfort during difficult times.
expanded:
The book of Psalms holds immense significance within the Old Testament. The Hebrew title, “Tehilim,” signifies “praises” or “hymns,” reflecting its primary function as a collection of songs expressing devotion to God. JFB notes that sixty-five psalms are designated by the inscription “mizmoi,” meaning “ode” or “song.”

The Psalms offer a profound insight into the human experience, encompassing joy and sorrow, hope and despair, all within the context of faith. As Barnes explains, they often use metaphors drawn from everyday life, such as Psalm 23’s depiction of God as a shepherd caring for his flock – a comparison easily understood by those familiar with pastoral life.

The commentary highlights that the Psalms are not merely expressions of personal feeling but also serve as instruction and guidance. Barnes emphasizes that “Thy word is a lamp unto my feet,” illuminating the path and preventing spiritual stumbling, while JFB adds that this light is fueled by the Holy Spirit. Colossians 3:16 further reinforces this idea, stating that the “word of Christ” should dwell richly within believers, informing their teaching, admonishment, psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs.

Ultimately, the Psalms provide a timeless resource for connecting with God through prayer, praise, and reflection. They offer solace in times of trouble and inspire hope amidst adversity, demonstrating the enduring power of faith expressed through music and poetry.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Thy word is a lamp unto my feet - This begins a new portion of the psalm, indicated by the Hebrew letter Nun (נ n), equivalent to our "n." The idea is, that the word of God is like a torch or lamp ton man in a dark night. It shows him the way; it prevents his stumbling over obstacles...

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: The Hebrew title of this book is Tehilim ("praises" or "hymns"), for a leading feature in its contents is praise, though the word occurs in the title of only one Psalm (the hundred forty-fifth).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: Let the word of Christ - The doctrine of Christ. Dwell in you richly in all wisdom - Abundantly, producing the spirit of true wisdom.

question: What is wisdom in the Bible?
short_answer: In the Bible, wisdom begins with reverence and awe of God, recognizing His holiness and power. It involves trusting in Him rather than relying on one's own understanding and seeking guidance when facing trials.
expanded:
The concept of wisdom within a biblical context is multifaceted, but consistently points to a reliance on divine guidance. Proverbs 1:7 establishes that the foundation of true wisdom lies in “the fear of the Lord,” which signifies reverence and awe in God’s presence – not a tormenting fear, but one born from recognizing His infinite nature. This initial step sets the stage for understanding and living according to God's principles.

James 1:5 expands on this by addressing the need for wisdom during trials and tribulations. It emphasizes that if individuals lack this divine wisdom, they should actively seek it through prayer. The highest form of wisdom governs patience in both poverty and riches, demonstrating a reliance on God’s will rather than human strategies.

Proverbs 3:5 further clarifies that true wisdom is found in trusting the Lord with one's heart and avoiding leaning on one's own understanding. This highlights the importance of humility and recognizing limitations when navigating life's complexities. Ultimately, biblical wisdom isn't about intellectual prowess but a deep relationship with God and a commitment to following His guidance.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The beginning of wisdom is found in the temper of reverence and awe...this fear has no torment, and is compatible with child-like love. But this and not love is the 'beginning of wisdom.'

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 'The fear of the Lord—the principle of true piety… beginning—first part, foundation.'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: If any of you lack wisdom - Probably this refers particularly to the kind of wisdom which they would need in their trials, to enable them to bear them in a proper manner…

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: 'Trust … heart—This is the center and marrow of true wisdom…The positive duty has its corresponding negation in the admonition against self-confidence.'

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But of him - That is, by his agency and power. It is not by philosophy; not from ourselves; but by his mercy.

question: Who is God the Father?
short_answer: God the Father is acknowledged as the Author of all things and sustains a paternal relationship to His creation. He is recognized by Christians as one God, distinct from but intimately connected with Jesus Christ.
expanded:
The concept of God the Father is central to Christian theology, and is presented in several passages within the New Testament. The Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:9) serves as a model for how believers should approach God, emphasizing a relationship of reverence and petition. Paul’s writings highlight that Christians acknowledge “one God, the Father” (1 Corinthians 8:6), who is the source of all existence and to whom believers live.

The apostle Paul further clarifies this understanding in his letter to the Romans, contrasting the spirit of bondage experienced under the Law with the spirit of adoption received through faith (Romans 8:15). This adoption allows believers to “cry, Abba, Father,” signifying a deeply personal and intimate relationship with God.  The prayer recorded in John 17 provides further insight, revealing Jesus’s profound connection with the Father and demonstrating the intimacy of their shared purpose.

Ephesians 1:3 introduces God as “the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ,” emphasizing His role in the divine plan for salvation and highlighting that believers are also brought into this familial relationship through faith in Christ. This understanding portrays God not merely as a distant creator, but as a loving parent who actively engages with humanity.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But to us - Christians. We acknowledge but one God; Whatever the pagan worship, we know that there is but one God; and he alone has a right to rule over us.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: God the Father is the end for whom and for whose glory believers live.

question: What is the Trinity?
short_answer: The Trinity refers to God existing as three distinct persons – Father, Son (Jesus Christ), and Holy Spirit – who are one God. This concept is revealed through scripture and demonstrated by their interconnected actions and roles in salvation.
expanded:
The doctrine of the Trinity affirms that God exists eternally as three distinct persons: the Father, the Son (Jesus Christ), and the Holy Spirit. These are not separate gods but rather three expressions of one divine being, united in essence and purpose. Matthew 28:19 highlights this by commanding disciples to baptize in "the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost," emphasizing their equal standing and association.

The order of these persons is significant; as JFB notes, “The grace of Christ comes first, for it is only by it we come to 'the love of God' the Father.” This illustrates a relationship within the Trinity where each person contributes uniquely to God’s plan. John 1:1 establishes the eternal existence of the Word (later identified as Jesus), indicating that even before creation, this divine being existed alongside God the Father.

The Holy Spirit is also integral to the Trinitarian understanding.  Matthew 3:16 describes Jesus' baptism where he “went up straightway out of the water,” accompanied by the descent of the Holy Spirit, demonstrating a simultaneous manifestation of all three persons. 1 Peter 1:2 speaks of God’s foreknowledge and foreordination, suggesting that this Trinitarian structure was part of His eternal plan.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: This verse contains what is usually called the apostolic benediction - the form which has been so long, and which is almost so universally used, in dismissing religious assemblies.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: "The grace of Christ comes first, for it is only by it we come to 'the love of God' the Father (Joh 14:6)."

question: What is election?
short_answer: Election refers to God’s eternal purpose and decree to choose certain individuals for salvation in Christ before the foundation of the world. It signifies that God's choice is not random but a deliberate act based on His own will and design.
expanded:
The doctrine of election centers around the idea that God, from all eternity, has chosen specific individuals to be saved. Ephesians 1:4 highlights this concept, stating that blessings were bestowed upon Christians “according to an eternal purpose.” Barnes explains that these blessings are not accidental but result from God's intentional design.  Romans 8:29 further elaborates on this by introducing the term 'foreknowledge,' suggesting God’s awareness and pre-ordained plan for those who would believe.

This doesn't imply a disregard for human responsibility; rather, it emphasizes God’s sovereignty in initiating salvation. John 15:16 clarifies that believers were not the ones who chose God, but He chose them to be His apostles, demonstrating His disinterested love and purpose.  2 Thessalonians 2:13 reinforces this by stating God “hath from the beginning chosen you to salvation,” emphasizing the eternal nature of this choice.

The concept is often misunderstood, with some interpreting it as a denial of free will. However, commentators like Barnes clarify that while God’s desire is for all men to be saved (1 Timothy 2:4), His ultimate plan and purpose involve selecting those who will ultimately receive salvation through faith in Christ.  This understanding aligns with the idea that God's promise isn't delayed due to a lack of power, but rather because He allows time for the full number of the elect to come to faith (2 Peter 3:9).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: The general sense of the passage is, that these blessings pertaining to heaven were bestowed upon Christians in accordance with an eternal purpose. They were not conferred by chance or hap-hazard. They were the result of intention and design on the part of God.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: It is in Him, and by virtue of union to Him...believers have all their blessings (Eph 3:11).

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: That God had chosen or elected them (εἵλετο heileto) to salvation. The doctrine of election, therefore, is true.

sources.author: JFB
sources.quote: Does 'foreknow' ...cannot be restricted to a mere foresight of future events, or acquaintance with what is passing here below.

sources.author: Barnes
sources.quote: But the word is often used to denote a desire, wish, or what is in accordance with the nature of anyone.
